## Energy Task Questions 1.4.20.docx

*File: `Energy Task Questions 1.4.20.docx`*

Energy Task Questions 1/4/20

Agenda:

Confirm the total value estimates

ERF total value:

$14.17 per tonne of abatement in July 2019 auction

Estimate of 13,860-22,344 tonnes of CO2 saved per year from FE04 HVAC Energy Efficiency Report.

Potential total value for 7-year project: $1,374,773.4-$2,216,301.36

This relies on the assumption that the non-upgraded fans are eligible as ‘market average fans of the same type and power’ and can therefore be used as a benchmark.

ESS total value:

Electricity certificate conversion factor=1.06

Gas certificate conversion factor=0.39

Lifetime: Energy savings are assumed to persist at a constant level for the expected lifetime of the CCAC (closed control air conditioner). Lifetime for this class is 10 years.

Assume cooling capacity is 1 (equal)

4th dot point, read Decision Regulation Impact Statement.

*put in savings from downer report…what they deemed

*to be calculated

Notes: 

Certificate number must be rounded down

Review the F2015L01712 legislation and confirm the calculation method if we are using the federal scheme

Chosen ERF calculation method: 

Energy Efficiency Methods; Refrigeration and ventilation fans subsection

Resource: http://www.cleanenergyregulator.gov.au/ERF/Pages/Choosing%20a%20project%20type/Opportunities%20for%20industry/Energy%20efficiency%20methods/Refrigeration-and-ventilation-fans.aspx

How to calculate abatement for High efficiency fan installations: abatement is determined by comparing the power consumption of the installed fan with the power consumption of a market average fan of the same fan type and power, with the same installation category.

F2015L01712 legislation Review:

To my knowledge, our fan does meet the requirements.

Does meet ISO 9001

Net abatement amount

Abatement = Abatement (High Efficiency Fans) + Abatement (Small Motor Fans)

Net abatement for installed HE Fans:

(Sum for each reporting period)

The abatement amount for each fan is determined by comparing the energy consumption of that fan with the energy consumption of a market average fan of the same type and power and is proportional to the reduction in energy consumption of the fan as compared to this benchmark.

Formula for calculating our fan is in Section 27 of document: https://www.legislation.gov.au/Details/F2015L01712 

Legislature questions to answer:

Does fan have a motor input power that is: greater or equal to 0.125 kilowatts and less than or equal to 185 kilowatts

Review and structure the draft presentation

Uncompleted.

Additional questions:

1. Your notes say for EFR, "ACCUs are considered ‘delivered’ under carbon abatement contract when they have been transferred from your ANREU account into the Clean Energy Regulator’s”.  How do ACCUs get into our ANREU account?   What is that process?

To claim the unit, the Emissions Reduction Fund Project Report and Crediting Application form must be completed and submitted for the reporting period. These are assessed within 90 days once received.

Once the claim for ACCUs is approved, an abatement statement from the Clean Energy Regulator will be received advising how many ACCUs your project is entitled to receive for the reporting period and the ACCUs will be transferred into your ANREU account. 

Resources:

Claim units: http://www.cleanenergyregulator.gov.au/ERF/Pages/Want%20to%20participate%20in%20the%20Emissions%20Reduction%20Fund/Step%204-Delivery%20and%20payment/Claiming-units.aspx

Delivery and payment: http://www.cleanenergyregulator.gov.au/ERF/Want-to-participate-in-the-Emissions-Reduction-Fund/Step-4-Delivery-and-payment

2. Your notes for ESS say that the Project Impact Assessment with Measurement and Verification Method can be forward created for 10 years, what is the estimated $ value for our project under this method?

This would probably be the most advantageous to us and can be calculated via several methods. These 

Just beginning to dive into the literature. The $ value of the implementation and amount of credits that can be received must be calculated by the Measurement and Verification method professional.

Researching what is required to qualify as this. There is a list of registered professionals (categorised on the type of work as it is work-specific) on the ESS website.

3. Are you able to try to draw a process diagram with the steps for each method (ERF and ESS) … you may like to experiment with the tool at websequencediagrams.com

See below

Energy Reduction Fund Process

Energy Savings Scheme Process

FE04: HVAC Energy Efficiency – Hardware                                                                                                                                4
© Downer 2020                                                                                                                                Commercial in Confidence
1               HVAC Energy Efficiency Hardware Fleet Enhancement
1.1             Executive Summary
The train’s Climate Control System (CCS) has one of the most significant impacts to customer and driver
comfort. The current HVAC units on the Waratah and SGT Fleet are a proven design with a sound
performance and reliability history. However, from an operational cost and environmental impact
perspective, the amount of energy used to operate the Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC)
system is a significant factor.
The HVAC hardware design has been in service for more than 10 years utilising technology that by
today's standards, is no longer energy efficient. Over this period the technology and reliability of rolling
stock HVAC units and their hardware components have improved allowing Downer to offer an
enhancement that will deliver significant benefits in energy efficiency and optimisation.
Downer and our HVAC supply partner Sigma have analysed operational service data logs to develop
simulation models using today's latest proven energy-efficient technology. The study showed significant
improvements in energy efficiency with a corresponding reduction in greenhouse gases with these new
components. These outcomes can be achieved without affecting the system cooling/heating
performance or modifying temperature control strategies.
Downer and Sigma propose three hardware enhancements that can be implemented as part of a
Hardware Energy Efficient HVAC (HEE HVAC) without major impact to the HVAC configuration or
performance. These are:
1. Replacing the existing condenser coil with a coil utilising 5mm diameter tubing;
2. Replacing the existing condenser fan with EC motor technology;
3. Replacing existing supply air fan with EC motor technology.
Based on the simulations, Sydney Trains can save between 16.5 and 26.6 GWh in energy per year
across the Waratah and SGT Fleets (119) with a corresponding reduction in emissions equivalent to
13,860 – 22,344 tonnes of CO2.
Further energy savings have been identified through making HVAC software control updates, and these
will be outlined in Fleet Enhancement proposal FE04 HVAC Energy Efficiency – Software.

1.2             Scope
This proposal outlines the program of work to complete the design and verification of the hardware
design. This will be achieved through:
   Finalising the engineering, designing and building the HEE HVAC prototype;
   Testing that the prototype design meets the required energy performance outcomes in a controlled
    climate chamber;
   Running an operational trial on an SGT Set over a 6-month period.
This work will verify the proposed solution:
   Achieves the targeted energy savings;
   Can be cost effectively upgraded;
   Maintains or exceeds specified heating and cooling performance;
   Achieves the required reliability in-service;
   Can deliver similar energy savings for Millennium through component hardware upgrades.


FE04: HVAC Energy Efficiency – Hardware                                                                            5

Downer TLS business, to optimise the upgrade cost and minimise the impact on current and long-term
TLS maintenance regimes.
Viable upgrade paths will be detailed during the first 2 months of the Design Phase with the most
immediate opportunity being the planned Millennium overhaul.

2               Benefits
2.1             Benefit Wheel
Downer has summarised the outcomes of each of the Fleet Enhancements using a Benefit
Wheel. Where the Fleet Enhancement provides a benefit in that particular area, the outcome is
highlighted. The Benefit Wheel for FE04 is illustrated in Figure 1.
The Benefit Wheel has been developed through:
   Engagement with key Sydney Trains and TfNSW stakeholders at working and executive levels;
   Review of relevant Sydney Trains and TfNSW documentation, for example:
         Sydney Trains Corporate Plan;
         TfNSW Customer Satisfaction Index;
         TfNSW Customer Personas;
   Downer internal workshops and understanding of best practice asset management systems (e.g. ISO
    55001).




                                          Figure 1: FE04 HVAC Benefit Wheel


The Benefit Wheel has been developed through:
   Engagement with key Sydney Trains and TfNSW stakeholders at working and executive levels;


FE04: HVAC Energy Efficiency – Hardware                                                                     7
© Downer 2020                                                                         Commercial in Confidence
   Review of relevant Sydney Trains and TfNSW documentation e.g.:
         Sydney Trains Corporate Plan;
         TfNSW Customer Satisfaction Index;
         TfNSW customer Personas.
   Downer internal workshops and understanding of best practice asset management systems (e.g. ISO
    55001).
Service availability for Sydney Trains customers is the underlying theme for all proposed Fleet
Enhancements. This is achieved through application of key asset management themes of Cost, Risk and
Performance as informed by the application of lifecycle management standard ISO 55001.
   Cost: Fleet Enhancement provides a financial benefit through a cost saving or revenue
    improvement;
   Risk: Fleet Enhancement reduces risk associated with fleet and service availability;
   Performance: Improves asset performance when utilised in fleet operations.
Outside the central theme of asset management, more specific benefits categories have been identified
as follows and have the associated meanings:
   Strategy: Fleet Enhancement enables strategy for Sydney Trains or TfNSW;
   Customer Satisfaction (CSI): Fleet Enhancement provides an increase in customer satisfaction
    index; the system will work better;
   Personas: Fleet Enhancement aligns to TfNSW personas (Student, Worker, Disabled etc.);
   Emissions: Fleet Enhancement reduces CO2 emissions;
   Energy: Fleet Enhancement reduces energy consumption;
   Safety: Fleet Enhancement improves safety;
   Timetable Risk: Fleet Enhancement reduces the dwell time or improves on time running;
   Security: Fleet Enhancement improves security;
   Customer Platform Experience: Fleet Enhancement improves customer experience for those on the
    platform;
   Customer Train Experience and Amenity: Fleet Enhancement improves customer experience and
    amenity for those on the train;
   Future Proofing: Fleet Enhancement provides aspects of asset future proofing;
   Fleet Operations and Information: Fleet Enhancement improves operational fidelity and
    comprehensiveness of asset information.

     Passenger loads are from existing operational data but in practice will vary between trains.
Not included in the modelling, the predicted savings additional factors have been identified including:

     HVAC operations during stabling or when the doors are open at terminal stops;
     Trains operating in all network sectors where ambient temperatures vary;
     Peak and off-peak conditions operating in tunnels and above ground;
     Condenser coils efficiency will change in partial cooling operation9.
Given these factors, a minimum energy saving of 10% will be targeted for the trial.
With an expected range of between 10% to 16% across the fleet of 119 sets this will save between
$2.32 – $3.37 million for the fleet on electricity costs annually 10.

3               Technical Description
3.1             Enhancement Objectives
The HEE HVAC Fleet Enhancement is designed to seamlessly enhance the HVAC system, to achieve
four key objectives:
1. The HEE HVAC solution can be implemented on Waratah and SGT with no change to the train side
   configuration;
2. The predicted energy savings can be proven quantitatively;
3. There is no reduction in performance at any of the specified environmental condition;
4. The design improves or has no impact to the HVAC reliability.
Downer is proposing three component enhancements that can be implemented without major change to
the HVAC configuration:
    Replace the existing condenser coil with a coil utilising 5mm diameter tubing;
    Replace the existing condenser fan with EC motor technology;
    Replace existing supply air fan with EC motor technology.

3.2             Description of the Enhancement
A complete review of the HVAC components on both Waratah and SGT sets identified the three
hardware components listed in Table 5. These offered the biggest energy saving without impacting
overall performance and reliability. The enhancements are all contained internally within the current
space envelope as illustrated in Figure 2.

3.3             Performance Specification
The HVAC Performance Specification was reviewed with adjustments made to the performance targets
in which Sigma are required to achieve for power consumption. The changes in the HVAC specification
are reflected in CES02016 Rev- Climate Control System Fleet Enhancement Specification. None of the
changes impact the high-level contract requirements from the Waratah TPS or the SGT SPR.
As there is no downgrade in cooling capacity performance to the HVAC, Downer considers the Notice of
Concession SW 0114: 2016 applicable – that the entire ASA standard is to be replaced with the
requirements of the PPP Waratah Train Performance Specification.




FE04: HVAC Energy Efficiency – Hardware                                                                         13
© Downer 2020                                                                              Commercial in Confidence
4               Design Engineering
4.1             Interfaces (Other Systems and Sub-Systems)
The component upgrades are all internal to the HVAC unit. Therefore, the upgraded HEE HVAC
interfaces to other systems and Systems Breakdown Structure will remain the same across Waratah and
SGT.

4.2             Concept of Operations (Indicative)
The component upgrades are all internal to the HVAC unit with no impact at Train Level. Therefore,
Concept of Operations is not required for this Fleet Enhancement.

4.3             Changes to the Minimum Operating Standards (MOS)
The component upgrades do not change the functionality and performance of the HVAC during normal
operations and therefore have no impact to the Minimum Operating Standards.

4.4             Specialist Engineering Review Input (Initial Human Factors Review)
The component upgrades are all internal within the HVAC and therefore have no impact to Human
Factors.

4.5             Reliability
Based on the EC fan supplier responses, components have equivalent MTBF. Sigma have experience
with EC fans used for these functions on other projects.

4.6             Technical Dependencies and Constraints
The component upgrades currently do not have any significant technical dependencies and constraints.

4.7             Technical Assumptions Made as Part of the Enhancement Proposal
Three key technical assumptions have been discussed between Downer and Sigma:
1. There is a need to conduct cooling capacity type testing to verify the major component upgrades.
   Typing testing will be undertaken in Sigma’s climate chamber and ‘Similarity’ proposals will be
   provided to minimise on train testing. EMC Type Testing will be undertaken for the new components;
2. The condenser coil and condenser fan are considered one system and therefore both will be
   upgraded;
3. Whilst the EC motor fans provide immediate energy savings, the simulations only consider the
   current fixed speed application.

4.8             Detailed Design Engineering Scope
Minimal detailed design engineering required as the Fleet Enhancement focuses on straight replacement
of the major components.

4.9             Technical Risk Assessment
Further engineering scope and detailed design will only be required if Sydney Trains proceed with future
considerations highlighted in Section 7.

5               Implementation
5.1             Scope
The scope of the HEE HVAC Hardware proposal will be delivered in the Design Phase. The Design
Phase will include an operational trial on a SGT Set for a 6-month period.
The roadmap for implementation is illustrated in Figure 3.




                                          Figure 3: Design Phase Roadmap
Downer and Sigma have developed a low-risk implementation strategy to ensure that the transition of
hardware changes from the Baseline HVAC design to the new HEE HVAC design has minimal impact to
Sydney Trains operations and fleet availability.
The plan involves testing the new design as a prototype in the laboratory (Climate Chamber) under
controlled conditions from mid-2020 and if successful, operationally trialling 4 prototype units in-service
from Q3 2020.
The timing for the Design Phase assumes the Early Works Program (EWP) rolls into the Design Phase
from 3 February 2020 to optimise design delivery timeframes and maintain momentum.
If required, Downer proposes that the EWP is extended through February to continue developing the
SDR should the variation require a period of negotiation and/or clarification. The approved variation will
be adjusted to account for this period.
The design process will progress from SDR to DDR to manage the degree of change proposed. Downer
is not planning to complete a PDR as the design is internal to the HVAC and does impact the Train Level
Systems.
Once the performance predictions are proven under laboratory conditions, an operational trial will be
conducted to verify the performance in-service. The trials will be evaluated, and the results used to
inform completion of the Design Phase. Based on the success of the trial a Go/No Go decision will be
made on whether to continue, modify or cancel the remaining Design Phase work.
The key to minimising the cost of prototyping and operational trials is having access to spare HVAC units
to upgrade. The current working assumption is units are made available from the TLS pool. A total of 4
units are required to be made available for the prototype and trials. The units replaced will be returned to
the maintenance pool.


FE04: HVAC Energy Efficiency – Hardware                                                                         17
© Downer 2020                                                                              Commercial in Confidence
The installation upgrade path and Through Life Support (TLS) strategy is currently being reviewed to
optimise both timing and cost. They will be delivered through changes to the existing TLS Deeds. A
revision change will be made to this proposal to reflect the agreed approach.
For the Millennium Fleet the current planning assumption is the proposed HEE HVAC design and trial
will inform the specification and design of the HVAC refurbishment planned during the upcoming
overhaul program.

5.2             Design and Trial
In the Design Phase Downer will:
    Finalise the engineering design;
    Build a prototype;
    Verify the prototype in Sigma’s climate chamber;
    Trial the HEE HVAC design on a revenue service Set over 6 months.
The Design Phase deliverables are outlined in Table 8 below.

5.3             Testing and Trials
5.3.1           Climate Chamber Testing (Factory Tests)
Climate chamber testing is planned to validate the proposed power reduction. An existing HVAC unit will
be upgraded by Sigma and then tested in a climate chamber.
Focusing on the key criteria that formed the basis of both the energy saving and environmental benefits
highlighted in section 2, the following parameters will be verified in the type test:
    Determination of refrigerant charge;

FE04: HVAC Energy Efficiency – Hardware                                                                          18
© Downer 2020                                                                               Commercial in Confidence
     Total supply of air flow per unit;
     Condenser air flow;
     Performance compared to the baseline;
     Power consumption.
Test instructions that will be considered in full or partial for climate chamber testing includes the items in
Table 9.
Table 9: Climate chamber testing suit of test instructions
    CTI                         Description
    CTI00735-                   CN01 HVAC Unit R&D Type Test
    CTI00929E                   CN01 EMC Type Test for PPP AC Unit

The HEE HVAC prototype will undergo type-level testing where document CTR00735-001- CN01 HVAC
Unit R&D Type Test can be used as the baseline performance reference. Testing and comparison of
the units will provide evidence for similarity of the EE HVAC performance on the train. Extensive
performance testing on the train will not be required.

5.3.2           Static Train Trials
Upgrades will be made to a further 3 existing HVAC units and together with the first prototype installed
across one Motor and one Trailer SGT Cars. These static trials will validate the operation of the HEE
HVAC units for installation on the train and before the train enters service. The single train will have HEE
HVAC units and baseline units installed for direct comparison.
Test instructions that will be considered in full or partial for static train trials include the items in Table 10.
Table 10: Static train trial suite of test instructions
    CTI                         Description
    CTI01583E                   CN13 Climate Control Saloon Delivery Routine Test

5.3.3           Operational Train Trials (In-Service)
Following static trials, in-service trials will validate HVAC power consumption and ongoing operation in
actual operational scenarios.
Data collected from the HEE HVAC and Baseline HVAC installed on the same train will enable a direct
comparison of the results since the single train will experience the same environmental variables.
Analysis of on-train power data will be provided by Downer’s own TrainDNA software and additional data
logging hardware installed on monitored HVACs. Data collected will include:
     HVAC unit status;
     Power consumption.
With TfNSW and Sydney Trains' support, Downer will run operational trials over a 6-month period to
include one transitional season and one summer period. The HEE HVAC will operate in all operational
settings (peak/off-peak; running/terminal/stabling; 20-24/7) and across a wide range of environmental
conditions (city/suburban temperatures; tunnels/above ground).
Additional performance monitoring equipment may be installed to support the trial.
Test instructions that will be considered in full or partial for in-service trials include the items in Table 11.



FE04: HVAC Energy Efficiency – Hardware                                                                               19
© Downer 2020                                                                                    Commercial in Confidence
Table 11: Operational train trial suite of test instructions
 CTI                            Description
 CTI01860- (New)                Fleet Enhancement Climate Control Energy Consumption (to be prepared by
                                TRR)
 CTI01598E                      CN13 Dynamic Track Trial Routine Test

At the end of the operational trials, Downer, Sigma and TfNSW/Sydney Trains will make a full
assessment and evaluation of performance against the trial objectives before proposing any wider full
fleet implementation upgrade.

5.3.4           Abatements
Any abatements due to performance issues in relation to the HEE HVAC trials shall be waived during the
trial period. Should a prototype unit fail or not perform as expected in service, the trial will be cancelled
for review and the original units re-installed.

5.4             Delivery and Pricing
Downer submitted a full commercial strategy and ROM pricing for the development, installation and TLS
for the Fleet and Set upgrade in its September submission.
Full Lifecycle ROM pricing estimates have been provided to allow TfNSW/Sydney Trains development of
the Business Case.
A number of the FEs were selected to be taken forward and further developed through the Fleet
Enhancement Early Works Program (EWP).
The EWP, through engagement with both TfNSW and Sydney Trains stakeholders have further refined
the requirements and scope of the selected Fleet Enhancements. For the HVAC Hardware proposal
Downer has provided the following pricing.
Design Phase:
Fixed price to complete the Design Phase including operational trials of the proposed solution. This price
includes:
     Meeting the requirements of the Systems Engineering Lifecycle – ASA T MU 06006 ST Systems
      Engineering V2.0 as defined in the SGT management plans;
     Project management;
     Engineering design;
     SDR, DDR, TRR and SVR design pack submission, review and approvals;
     Sigma Non-Recurring Engineering;
     HVAC trial design and management;
     Prototype materials;
     Upgrading 4 existing HVAC units;
     Trial evaluation;
     Installation strategy and planning;
     Supply chain management;

© Downer 2020                                                                              Commercial in Confidence
     Commercial management.

5.5             Delivery and Pricing Assumptions
The pricing for the Design Phase is supported by the following assumptions:
     Approval to commence the Design Phase and engage suppliers is given in February 2020 as a
      continuation of the EWP. First Design Phase milestone to be completed is SDR;
     TfNSW/Sydney Trains appoint a project lead and appropriate team for the Design Phase;
     Downer and the TfNSW team work in a collaborative manner with ongoing reviews and
      assessments;
     Downer will not conduct PDR due to the nature of the design;
     Materials for prototype are ordered before the end of February 2020;
     1 spare HVAC unit is made available through TLS Deed to build a protype in June 2020;
     3 spare HVAC units are made available through TLS Deed for upgrade in Q3 2020;
     Operational trial set is made available under the SGT TLS Deed from the end of September 2020;
     4 prototype units are installed on an SGT Set. The units removed will be placed in the rotable
      spares pool;
     On completion of a successful trial the units will be left on the set (assuming the results are
      validated);
     On completion of a successful trial, a design proposal to upgrade to a HEE HVAC for the Millennium
      Fleet will be provided noting the units are of an earlier generation.

5.6             Delivery Strategy Under Each Contract
The Fleet Enhancement works will be performed under several contracts across the three fleets:
   SGT Delivery Deed: Overall FE project delivery and Design Phase activities up to Systems
    Verification Review (SVR), including any trials in this period, will be performed under the SGT
    Delivery Deed;
   SGT TLS Deed: Installation of HVAC upgrade and ongoing TLS activities in relation to specific SGT
    Fleet installed scope will be performed under the SGT TLS Deed;
   Waratah TLS Deed: Installation of HVAC upgrade and ongoing TLS activities in relation to the
    specific Waratah Fleet installed scope will be performed under Waratah TLS Deed.
The Design Phase for FE04 includes all design and development activities, including trials on an SGT
Set (Trial Set) prior to SVR. It is the supplier’s understanding that Sydney Trains will provide access to
Trial Set at the required time.
For Waratah, Downer is not the direct contracting entity with Sydney Trains under the Waratah PPP
Contract. Sydney Trains will be responsible for managing Reliance Rail Pty Ltd (Reliance Rail) to allow
for installation of the Fleet Enhancement.
Sydney Trains will be required to issue a variation to Reliance Rail requesting implementation of the
Fleet Enhancement. Reliance Rail will then issue a variation under the TLS Contact with Downer to
perform the implementation works. Downer has not made any allowance for reviews or obtaining
approvals by Reliance Rail. Equally, it is important to note that the pricing provided within this proposal
excludes any further costs and margin that Reliance Rail may seek to add.

7               Future Opportunities
7.1             Millennium Train Consideration
The same energy efficiency enhancement process that was undertaken with the Waratah and SGT
Fleets can be applied to the Millennium Fleet. As the HVACs are a generation behind it is expected to
yield more energy savings.
In some cases, the hardware and material design will be different.
Downer highlights that the Millennium fleet HVAC 16-year major overhaul is imminent. This presents the
best opportunity to consider component upgrades for this fleet. The upgrades will require minimal testing
after being proven in-service on the SGT Sets through this proposal.

7.2             Future Considerations
All equipment in the Climate Control System has been holistically reviewed for optimisation to realise the
energy saving. Further energy efficiency could be gained in other areas of the Climate Control System
as outlined in Table 12.
Based on stakeholder feedback, components 1 (Duct Heaters), 4 (Reduce Fresh Air Intake While Doors
are Open) and 6 (Overnight Operation), are detailed in a separate proposal FE04 HVAC Energy
Efficiency – Software for future consideration.

Overview:

From the FE04 HVAC Energy Efficiency Report there are two main advantages to the climate system in terms of savings; reduction of carbon emission and reduction of energy consumption. These four initiatives cater towards these advantages and together, allow Downer to maximise the reward for implementing these changes in the Waratah fleet.

The FED Emissions Reduction Fund rewards businesses for a reduction in CO2 emission by awarding an Australian Carbon Credit Unit (ACCU) per tonne of Carbon Dioxide Equivalent (tCO2-e) stored or avoided by the project. These Carbon Credits can then be sold to the government via a carbon abatement contract or sold on the secondary market. Via this method, an additional revenue stream of $166,320 to $268,128 could be added as a result of the HVAC upgrade. These prices are indicative and can vary depending on the demand set by the government via a carbon abatement contract, or the supply and demand in the secondary market.

A carbon abatement contract is not required to participate in the Emissions Reduction Fund. ACCUs can be issued to a project without one and sold on the market. Alternatively, a contract can be secured later via participation in an online auction.

This scheme is advantageous as it provides quantitative methods of calculating the additional revenue stream while offering multiple markets and potentially prices for these ACCUs to be sold. This initiative does not provide any form of upfront funding.

It should be noted that eligibility for this fund relies on three main requirements: the project does not commence before it has been registered with the Clean Energy Regulator and a response has been received in writing; the project is not required to be undertaken by or under a Commonwealth, State, or Territory Law; the project is not being undertaken under another Commonwealth, State or Territory government program in the absence of registration under the Emissions Reduction Fund. A Forward Abatement Estimate covering the crediting period for the project is required to secure a carbon abatement contract.

A Crediting period is the timeframe a project is able to claim ACCUs and is 7 years for FE04 HVAC project type. The Reporting period is the timeframe for which a report is prepared for submission to the Clean Energy Regulator and is at discretion of the individual responsible for the project. Maximum reporting period for emissions avoidance projects is every 2 years.

With these (and several other) requirements satisfied, the project outlined in the FE04 HVAC Energy Efficiency Report is suitable for this form of funding and should be applied for as ‘reductions in emission intensity of transport is a valid project type.

The Clean Energy Innovation Fund is the largest dedicated Australian investor in its sector that targets technologies and businesses beyond the research and development stage which can benefit from early stage seed of growth capital to help them progress to the next stage of their development. The fund uses CEFC financing to provide primarily equity finance to innovative clean energy projects and business which involve renewable, energy efficiency and low emissions technologies. 

The Sustainable Cities Investment Program as a subsection of this fund invests to accelerate the development and deployment in clean energy projects in Australia’s 50 largest cities, having committed 2.9 billion to 5000 Sustainable Cities opportunities as of 30 June 2019. Finance can be used across the urban environment to delivery clean energy benefits to a range of sector including low emissions transport and energy efficient large-scale economic infrastructure, and to encourage the switch to energy efficient vehicles.

The eligibility of the project and amount of funding is at the discretion of the CEFC and can take the form of direct investments, investments in investment funds, and specialist asset finance programs. Financing varies from $10,000 to 5 million for small scale projects and $20 million and above for large-scale projects and each opportunity is considered on a case by case basis. 

In scope of the FE04 HVAC Energy Efficiency Report, this source of funding would be advantageous as an upfront method of minimising the costs before the project begins. However, funds cannot be estimated, and it is uncertain whether the HVAC upgrade projects fits with the Clean Energy Innovation Fund’s ethos which mainly targets businesses that are not yet established or of sufficient maturity, size or otherwise ready to attract sufficient private sector capital.

Consequently, the FE04 HVAC Energy Efficiency is eligible as an Australian-based investment that is attempting to implement energy efficient and low emission technologies. However, the nature of the project and Downer as a company is less compatible with the fund’s investing strategy and eligibility is therefore unlikely.

Energy Performance Contracting (EPC) relies on contracting an Energy Service Company (ESCO) to evaluate the defined project and its scope, and provide recommendations into cost-effective improvements, work to implement recommendations, and guarantee the resulting savings will cover all project costs. The Energy Performance Contract is negotiated with the Energy Service Company and the savings from the recommendations cover the project costs within 3 to 7 years.

This form of finance provides beneficial upfront funding which can lower/eliminate the HVAC upgrade installation costs. Funds are allocated based on eligibility determined by the Energy Service Company.

The literature suggests that this source of funding is exclusively for a large property or portfolio of properties but should be confirmed by an ESCO.

NSW Energy Saving Scheme awards financial incentives for organisations investing in energy saving projects that achieve reduced energy consumption by installing, improving, or replacing energy savings equipment. An Accredited Certificate Provider offers energy saving certificates (currency in the scheme) for each notional MWh of energy that investments save which can be sold back to electricity retailers,* who are mandatory scheme participants and purchase these to meet their own legislated targets.

There are multiple calculation methods that are eligible [*does the one Downer used apply?] to determine the about of MWh saved. Each MWh saved is valued at one ESC.

Organisations register for this scheme via the ESS Registry, an online database which records the registration, transfer, and surrender of energy savings certificates.

*penalty must be paid if don’t surrender certificates by time

### Sheet: ERF

Section | Question | Comments | Status | Explanation

Application guide |  |  |  | 

 |  Is the project proposed to be carried out, or being carried out, entirely within Australia? | Under the requirements of the Act, all projects must be carried out in Australia including its external territories. | Known | 

 | Method category (selection) | To apply under the Refrigeration and Ventilation Fans method, you should select the ‘Energy Efficiency’ option in the drop-down menu. | Known | 

 | Method (selection) | Select ‘Refrigeration and Ventilation Fans’. | Known | 

 |  Does the project meet the newness requirement? | For a project to be declared an eligible project, the project must meet the newness requirement. You should ensure you have reviewed the newness requirements of section 27 (4A) to (4E) of the Act prior to selecting the appropriate response. | Known | Project is new

 |  Are the project activities funded under any of the government programs, or do they include any activities listed in section 21 of the Carbon Credits (Carbon Farming Initiative) Rule 2015 (rule)? | The Rule excludes certain activities funded by other government programs from being the subject of an eligible offsets project. The list of other government programs in section 21 of the Rule includes the Renewable Energy Target, the NSW Energy Savings Scheme, the Victorian Energy Efficiency Target and several other state and territory based energy efficiency schemes. Ensure that you review the requirements prior to selecting the appropriate response. | Known | 

 | Is the project, or any part of it, required to be carried out by or under a law of the Commonwealth, a State, or a Territory (regulatory additionality requirement)? | The Rule excludes certain activities funded by other government programs from being the subject of an eligible offsets project. The list of other government programs in section 21 of the Rule includes the Renewable Energy Target, the NSW Energy Savings Scheme, the Victorian Energy Efficiency Target and several other state and territory based energy efficiency schemes. Ensure that you review the requirements prior to selecting the appropriate response. | Known | 

 | Is the project, or any part of it, required to be carried out by or under a law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory (regulatory additionality requirement)? | For a project to be declared an eligible project, the project activities must not be required by law. This includes requirements of local development approvals and planning regulations, which are a requirement of state and territory law.
In addition to the regulatory additionality requirement, subsection 17(2) of the Method states that if a small motor fan upgrades activity is part of a construction or upgrade where development approval is required for the works, then it is not eligible under the Method, with the exception of circumstances where a development approval is required only because of the small motor fan upgrade (and not for other works). | Known | 

 | What start date do you wish to nominate for your project? | The start date of a project refers to the date the project crediting period will begin. It does not refer to the date the activities take place. For some projects, you may choose a date a few months after the activities have taken place when the project is fully operational and is generating abatement.
The start date for a Refrigeration and Ventilation Fans project may be on or up to 18 months after the date the project is declared eligible by the Clean Energy Regulator. When selecting this date be aware that legislatively the Clean Energy Regulator has 90 days to assess projects. You cannot select a start date earlier than the date the project is declared.
If you do not nominate a start date, the start date for your crediting period will be the day the project is declared eligible. | Known by Downer | 

 | What is the forward abatement estimate for the project? | The forward abatement estimate (FAE) is an estimate of the amount of carbon abatement, in tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent (t CO2-e), which corresponds to the number of Australian Carbon Credit Units (ACCUs) likely to be issued in relation to the project. The purpose of the FAE is to allow the Clean Energy Regulator to assign an audit schedule for your project. It is an estimate only and can be rounded to the nearest 50 000 t CO2-e over the crediting period for your project. More information can be found on the FAE2 section of the Clean Energy Regulator’s website. | Known | 13,860-22,344 CO2e each year. If project ends in 2025, then approximately 55,440-89,376 tonnes CO2e. However, if audit schedule is assuming it would be 7 years, 97,020-156,408 (100,000-150,000 with rounding)

 |  Crediting Period (years) of the project? | The crediting period for this method is 7 years. This field will be pre-populated and fixed. | Known | For our project type

 | Average Annual FAE (forward abatement estimate)? | The average annual FAE is the FAE / 7 (the crediting period). This field will be pre-populated based on the forward abatement estimate provided. | Known | Pre-populated

 | What is the estimate of the peak period of the project or abatement | This information will be considered when determining the timing of the project’s scheduled audits. | Unknown | Not included in document but could be generally stated as December, January, February (summer)?

 | Does the project require any regulatory approvals? | You must declare any regulatory approvals that will be (or were) required to undertake the project activities. For example, commissioning a significant plant extension is likely to require state government development and operational approvals. | Known by Downer | 

 | Have all relevant regulatory approvals been obtained for the project (if required)? | You do not need to have obtained all regulatory approvals when you apply, however, to be eligible for ACCUs, your project must have received all the required regulatory approvals by the end of the first reporting period.
If you have received regulatory approvals for the project, you should include them in your application. If regulatory approvals have not been received or copy of approval has not been provided to the Clean Energy Regulator, may result in a delay in issuance of ACCUs. | Known by Downer | 

 | Where is the physical location of the project or how will the project boundary be defined | The Rule requires that information identifying the location of the project must be provided. You must provide the location details for at least one fan installation, modification or upgrade. Different information is required for identified buildings or sites than for aggregated projects where not all the buildings or sites in the project have been identified. Minimum of one identified site requirement:
To meet the requirements of the assessment process you will be required to demonstrate that you have the legal right to carry out the project for (at least) one building/address/location. Therefore you must provide at least one address/location in your response to this question.
If you are aware that there may be more Emissions Reduction Fund projects that are taking place at that site, or activities supported by other government programs, you should either specify the exact location of your project, or alert the Clean Energy Regulator of these activities so that they can be separated from the project (see section 15A of the Act) – No double counting test and (see section 21 of the Rule – co-location of projects).
If there are multiple locations involved in the project, provide the addresses in a structured format, either in
the address reporting template3 provided on the Clean Energy Regulator website4 or consistent with AS4590 56  | Known | 

 | Describe the project and activities | You must provide the project activity details required by section 11 of the Method. Different information is required for identified fans than for aggregated projects where not all fans in the project have been identified. In your response you should provide a detailed description of what the project will entail, and how the project meets the eligibility requirements of Parts 2 and 3 of the Method.
Where you know the fan to be installed, modified or replaced, a description of the activities must be provided, including:
The nature of the activity, whether it be installation, modification or upgrade, or a combination of these.
For a refrigeration fan, the type of refrigeration system the fan will be installed in (from section 11 of the Method and as defined in section 5) and whether the fan is a condenser fan, evaporator fan or a fan installed in a cooling tower that provides heat rejection for a refrigeration system (from section 27 of the Method).
For a ventilation fan, the type of building the fan will service (from schedule 2 of the Method) and whether the fan is a heating/cooling ventilation, if so, the NCC climate zone for the building’s location (from schedule 2 of the Method and as defined in section 5).
The type of activity, high efficiency fan installation or small motor fan upgrade, the fan will undertake and its associated characteristics (from part 2 of the Method).
The motor input power and as appropriate the brand, manufacturer and model number of the fan.                          
Where you do not know the fans to be installed, modified or replaced that will be included in your project at the point of application, you can instead provide a description of the ‘class’. This is a description of the types of fans you will be installing, modifying or upgrading as part of your project and the types of refrigeration systems or buildings where these will occur. These items must all be as listed in Part 3 and schedules at the end of the method.
For aggregated projects or projects which involve installing, modifying or upgrading many different types of fans this may involve a significant amount of information. To assist you in providing all of this information, the annex to this document provides an example template that you can use to describe the class of fan installation, modification or upgrade in your project. You can save the template as a separate document and upload it to your application in the client portal | Known | 

 | Describe the skill and expertise of any person intended to be used in carrying out the project consistently with the relevant method | Having people involved in your project who can comply with the rules and requirements of the method for calculations, monitoring, record keeping and reporting is critical to the success of your project, as you cannot claim ACCUs unless you comply with these requirements. In your response to this question the Clean Energy Regulator is looking for evidence that you have considered these requirements of the method and why the party undertaking these activities is qualified to do so.
The high efficiency fan installations activity in the method requires testing of fan parameters in accordance with ISO 5801 by either the fan manufacturer or the participant. If you decide to undertake fan testing, then a suitably skilled person may be required to carry out the tests, such as an experienced fan engineer. | Known by Downer | 

 |  Do you have the legal right to carry out the project? | You should read the information on legal right available on the Legal right7 page on the Clean Energy Regulator website and consider seeking professional legal advice before signing this form to ensure you have the legal right to carry out the project as required by the Act. Your response to this question should demonstrate that you have read and considered the issues identified in this advice.
You must provide evidence of your legal right to carry out the project at all sites listed on your application at the time of applying for registration. It is important to note that approval of the project by the Clean Energy Regulator does not establish legal right. You are required to keep records of any agreements with stakeholders. Legal right for all sites, activities and equipment in the project, including those added after registration, will be checked during audits. | Known by Downer | Waiting for approval on the project

Application for Part B (Method) |  |  |  |  |  |  | 

 | What method will you use? | 1 | What method to calculate the energy savings will be utilised? | Pending |  |  | 

IHEAB Method Guide |  |  |  |  | Proposed method as it analysis the Installation of High Efficiency Appliances for Business method (Business Appliances) is used to calculate electricity savings from the installation of high efficiency:
    Close control air conditioners
    Air conditioners
    Brushless DC motors to power fans in refrigerated display cabinets
    Brushless DC motors to power ventilation fans
    Motors powering various other equipment |  | 

Requirements | Energy saver | 3.1 | An ACP can only calculate energy savings and create ESCs if the ACP is the ‘energy saver’ under the ESS Rule. The ACP must be the energy saver as at the implementation date. An energy saver can be either:
the original energy saver – which, under the Business Appliances method, is the purchaser (discussed below), or the nominated energy saver – which is someone the original energy saver has nominated as the energy saver by completing a Nomination Form.
An ACP that is the original energy saver must be accredited as an ACP prior to the implementation date in order to create ESCs from an implementation.
An ACP that is a nominated energy saver must: be accredited as an ACP prior to the implementation date and before the nomination is made,
have a documented procedure for obtaining the nomination from the original energy saver, and be nominated by the original energy saver on or before the implementation date. The nomination is taken to occur on the date that the nomination form is signed by the original energy saver. | Unknown | Will this be Sydney trains or Downer? |  | 

 | Purchaser | 3.1.1 | In general, the purchaser is the person who purchases or leases the goods or services that enable the relevant energy savings to be made. However, the following persons cannot be a ‘purchaser’ and therefore cannot be an original energy saver under the Business Appliances method:
-an ACP who is not the owner, occupier or operator of the relevant site.
-a person who purchases or leases the goods or services for the purpose of reselling the end-user equipment, unless the resale will be an inclusion in a contract for the sale of land or a strata scheme lot. | Known |  |  | 

 | Implementation and implementation date | 3.2 | The ESS Rule defines ‘implementations’, ‘implementation date’ and ‘site’ (explained below). These concepts are used to determine the number of ESCs, and from when they can be created. | Known by Downer |  |  | 

 | Implementation | 3.2.1 | An implementation is the delivery of an energy saving activity (called a ‘RESA’ in the ESS Rule)9 at a site. Activities eligible to be RESAs where the Business Appliance Method is used are set out in Schedule F to the ESS Rule (see Appendices to this document). | Known by Downer |  |  | 

 | Implementation date | 3.2.2 | For ACPs that use the Business Appliances method, the implementation date is the date the appliance is installed. | Known by Downer |  |  | 

 | Site | 3.2.3 | Under the ESS Rule, a ‘site’ means the location of where the End-User Equipment was installed. This may be defined by:
-an address, or
-a unique identifier, as specified for the relevant Implementation that identifies the affected End-User Equipment; or
-a method accepted by the Scheme Administrator. | Known by Downer |  |  | 

 | Eligible applicances | 3.3 | Eligible appliances are those included in an activity definition in Schedule F of the ESS Rule. Each activity definition defines a class of appliance that can be installed and the requirements the appliance must meet. The activity definitions are listed in Table 3.3.1 of this guide. Includes: High efficiency close control air conditioner, High efficiency air conditioner, Electronically commutated (brushless DC) motor to power a ventilation fan, Three phase electric high efficiency motor | Known | *Double check needed |  | 

 | Equipment requirements | 3.3.1 | Equipment requirements are specified in each activity definition. For this method, the activity definitions are in Schedule F of the ESS Rule. These requirements may include:
-meeting the requirements of various Australian Standards
-being a Greenhouse and Energy Minimum Standards (GEMS) registered product10
-meeting Minimum Energy Performance Standards (MEPS)11
-meeting other performance requirements specified in Schedule F of the ESS Rule (reproduced in Appendix A of this guide), and
-any requirements specified by the Scheme Administrator. | Known by Downer | Downer will need to confirm |  | 

 | Checking the appliance meets the equipment requirements | 3.3.2 | A full list of equipment requirements for each activity definition are listed Schedule F of the ESS Rule and are reproduced in Appendix A of this guide.
We do not maintain a list of accepted products that meet the equipment requirements for this method. You can use the Commonwealth Government’s GEMS registry to determine if your appliance meets these requirements. However, a listing in the GEMS registry does not guarantee your appliance meets all the equipment requirements for the ESS, as certain activity definitions in the ESS Rule place additional requirements on appliances under this method, which must also be met for equipment to be installed under the ESS. | Known by Downer | Downer will need to confirm |  | 

 | Installation requirements | 3.4 | The key installation requirement is that the appliance must be installed at a site in NSW. There are also additional installation requirements for each appliance, as noted in Schedule F of the ESS Rule (reproduced in Appendix A of this guide). | Known |  |  | 

 | Efficiency requirement for installing new end-user equipment | 3.4.1 | The installation of ‘New End-User Equipment’ only constitutes a RESA if the Scheme Administrator is satisfied that it is more efficient than the average energy efficiency of End- User Equipment of the same type, or that it provides the same function, output or service. The Activity Definitions in Schedule F provide a range of options for the installation of New End-User Equipment, as well as the replacement, modification or removal of existing End- User Equipment or a particular component of existing End-User Equipment.
Where an Activity Definition in Schedule F of the ESS Rule involves the installation of New End-User Equipment it must have a higher efficiency than any baseline/defined parameters specified in Schedule F of the ESS Rule (eg, Activity Definition F1 – Install a new high efficiency refrigerated display cabinet).
However, where an Activity Definition in Schedule F involves the installation of a new component for the purpose of reducing the energy consumption of existing End-User Equipment, it does not necessarily need to consume less energy than End-User Equipment of the same type. This is because the component is being used to achieve an overall reduction in energy consumption from its influence on the existing EUE End-User Equipment (eg, Activity Definition F10 – Install an oxygen trim system on an existing boiler). | Known | Will be known by end of trials. Climate test indicates this will be satisfied... awaiting confirmation from further trials. |  | 

 | Minimum requirements for conduct of representatives | 3.5 | The Scheme Administrator has established minimum requirements for the conduct of ACPs and their representatives. This includes ACP responsibilities for:
-training representatives
-maintaining a register of representatives
-ensuring there is a formal, documented, signed and enforceable (legally binding) contract or agreement in place for each representative, and
-providing appropriate customer service.
ACPs are accountable for all ESS activities conducted by employees, third parties and other representatives. This includes all aspects of an activity for which they create ESCs, from the initial engagement with customers, through to the final quality assurance of documents. ACPs will be held responsible for all actions, omissions and information provided by representatives acting on their behalf under the ESS – regardless of any contract or agreement with other parties. For more information, refer to ESS Notice 01/2013 (amended July 2014) Minimum requirements for conduct of persons acting on behalf of ACPs.12 | Known by Downer |  |  | 

 | Electricity savings | 4 | The relevant equations and schedules used to calculate energy savings using the method are provided in the Appendices of this guide. Under the ESS Rule, energy savings comprise both ‘electricity savings’ and ‘gas savings’. | Known by Downer |  |  | 

Calculating energy savings |  | 4.1 | The electricity savings from an implementation of the method can be calculated using Equation 17 of the ESS Rule (reproduced in Appendix C of this guide), which uses:
the Deemed Equipment Electricity Savings from Schedule F of the ESS Rule (reproduced in Appendix A of this guide), and
the regional network factor from Table A24 in Schedule A of the ESS Rule (reproduced in Appendix B of this guide). | Pending |  |  | 

Calculating and creating ESCs |  | 5 | Equation 1 of the ESS Rule is used to calculate the number of ESCs that may be created from the energy savings calculated in relation to an implementation. Equation 1: Number of Certificates = ΣImplementations Electricity Savings x Electricity Certificate Conversion Factor + Gas Savings x Gas Certificate Conversion Factor | Pending |  |  | 

 | Applying to register ESCs | 5.1 (part 1) | Certain information must be submitted to the Scheme Administrator before an ACP applies to register ESCs.13 ACPs are to provide the required information by completing an
Implementation Data Sheet14 and submitting it through the ESS Portal.15 The Implementation Data Sheet will include a calculation of the number of ESCs to be created in accordance with Equation 1 in the ESS Rule. This calculation involves:
multiplying electricity savings (from Equation 17 in the ESS Rule) by the electricity certificate conversion factor (1.06)16
multiplying gas savings (from Equation 17 in the ESS Rule) by the gas certificate conversion factor (0.39),17 and
adding the two figures together.
The result is the total number of ESCs that ACPs can apply to register from the implementation or implementations. If the result is not a whole number, it is rounded down to the nearest whole number.
There are no restrictions on how many implementations can be bundled together in the same Implementation Data Sheet...  | Pending |  |  | 

 |  | 5.1  (part 2) | ... However:
-ACPs must apply to register all ESCs included in an Implementation Data Sheet in a single application
-ACPs cannot split energy savings calculated from a single implementation across two or more Implementation Data Sheets, and
-each Implementation Data Sheet must only include the calculation of energy savings that are taken to have occurred in the same calendar year (commonly referred to as ‘vintage’).
When determining how many implementations to bundle in the same Implementation Data Sheet, ACPs should consider:
the ESC creation limit specified in their Accreditation Notice, as they must be able to register all the ESCs in the bundle at the same time, and the cost of registering the ESCs.18
More information on applying to register the creation of ESCs can be found on ESS | Pending |  |  | 

Minimum required records | *See documentation | 6 |  |  |  |  |
