## PIAMV-Template-Preliminary-MV-Professional-Report-V1.1 Sydney Trains Jul 2021 copy.pdf

*File: `PIAMV-Template-Preliminary-MV-Professional-Report-V1.1 Sydney Trains Jul 2021 copy.pdf`*

Template: Preliminary M&V Professional
Report
PIAM&V Method


             Purpose of this template
The Energy Savings Scheme Rule of 2009 (ESS Rule) requires that Measurement and
Verification (M&V) Professionals be used to confirm the validity of energy models used by
Accredited Certificate Providers (ACPs) when implementing activities that use the Project
Impact Assessment with Measurement and Verification (PIAM&V) method.

ACPs must ensure that an approved M&V Professional deems appropriate the various
aspects of their measurement procedures, energy models and parameters in accordance
with the requirements of clause 7A of the ESS Rule and PIAM&V Method Requirements
published by IPART.

M&V Professionals must provide their validation of the baseline measurement procedures
and their written explanatory reasoning using this template.



             Instructions for using this template
To use this template, you must complete every section. The completed report will be
reviewed by PIAM&V auditors and must include sufficient reasoning to justify your opinion of
the various aspects of the measurement procedures relating to the baseline energy model.

Please note that you must validate the measurement procedures in relation to the baseline
energy model and complete the Preliminary M&V Professional Report with your written
explanatory reasoning prior to the implementation date.

Reports without sufficient explanatory reasoning may be assessed as not meeting the
requirements of the ESS Rule and/or PIAM&V Method Requirements published by IPART,
and may impact the validity of the energy savings certificates for the relevant
implementation.



             Document control
Version number       Change descriptions                                         Date published
V1.0                 Initial release                                             March 2020
V1.1                 Minor amendments to update formatting and fix text boxes.   October 2020




IPART Template – Preliminary M&V Professional Report (V1.1 October 2020)                          1/7
IPART Template - Preliminary M&V Professional Report (V1.1 October 2020)   2/7
Report details

 M&V Professional name                Collin Macpherson

 ACP name (company)                   Wattly Pty Ltd

 RESA name                            SGT train sets HVAC control software upgrade.
                                      Sydney Trains
 Implementation address               Greater Sydney

 Project description                  This project involves the upgrading of the control software
                                      that is used to run the HVAC systems on sets of 8 cars
                                      making up a train “set” on the Sydney Trains network
                                      operated by Sydney Trains. Control improvements will include
                                          - Duct heater optimisation
                                          - HVAC set back mode enabling.
                                      The improvements to the control software are expected to
                                      generate electricity savings in the train set HVAC systems of
                                      10% or more.
                                      No changes to operating hardware will occur as part of this
                                      RESA.
                                      The project expects to generate electricity savings of up to 25
                                      GWh per annum
 EUE included in the                  The EUE for this project is the fleet of SGT and Waratah
 measurement boundary                 electric train models operating on the Sydney Trains network.
                                      Due to the large number of train sets involved in this RESA
                                      the ACP has chosen to use the ESS Rule Section 7A.20
                                      Sampling Method for developing the total savings estimates
                                      for the RESA.
 Date of this report                  6/7/2021




IPART Template - Preliminary M&V Professional Report (V1.1 October 2020)                                3/7
Measurement Procedures – Baseline Energy Model
    Explain how the Measurement Procedures for measuring electricity/Gas consumption, Independent
    Variables, Site Constants and other parameters are appropriate for the Baseline Energy Model (in
    accordance with clause 7A.5 of the ESS Rule).
    The Measurement Procedures must:
         o      be based on a baseline Measurement Period that meets the requirements of clause 7A.5(a)-(b)1
         o      include any time periods in which Independent Variables may reasonably be expected to lead to the
                Implementation increasing electricity consumption or Gas consumption or both (clause 7A.5(f1))
         o      cover all relevant Independent Variables that impact the energy consumption of the End-User
                Equipment
         o      have sufficient frequency of measurements over the baseline Measurement Period to establish
                normal operating conditions (consider frequency of measurements in respect of power draw,
                availability of metered energy data and other relevant factors)
         o      define which items of End-User Equipment will have their electricity consumption, Gas
                consumption, or both, measured (the measurement boundary)
         o      specify measurement equipment (meters) or other sources of measurements
         o      define the calibration procedures, accuracy and precision of such measurement methods, and
         o      record and exclude any Non-Routine Events that occurred during the baseline Measurement
                Period, ensuring that the percentage of time excluded is less than 20% of the baseline
                Measurement Period

The measurement procedures used for measuring Electricity Consumption and monitoring Site
Constants are considered appropriate for the project and are in accordance with Clause 7A.5 of the
ESS Rule. This has been demonstrated by the ACP as follows:

7A.5(a)
The measurement period for the Baseline Model covers the period from 1/10/2020 to 30/9/2021, with
relevant data consolidated into daily totals. The daily totals for the HVAC electricity consumption will
be derived 10 second interval data logged at the individual HVAC system meters installed on each
train set (of 8 carriages).

7A.5(b) (i)
The Baseline Period is prior to the project implementation date which is scheduled for October 2021.

7A.5(b) (ii)
N/A

7A.5(b) (iii)
N/A

7A.5(b) (iv)
N/A

7A.5(c)
The measurement frequency for the RESA has been set at daily intervals, creating 365 data points for
the Baseline Model. There are not expected to be any non-routine events during the Baseline Period.
Where some interval data used to accumulate the daily totals is missing for some reason, the ACP
has decided to eliminate daily data if more than 20% of the interval data is missing.


1    If the end date of the baseline Measurement Period and/or the Implementation Date of the project have not
     yet been defined at the point this Preliminary M&V Professional Report is completed, please provide a
     validation of the planned dates and provide explanatory reasoning on whether the proposed Measurement
     Procedures are adequate to ensure the baseline Measurement Period will meet the requirements under
     clause 7A.5.




IPART Template - Preliminary M&V Professional Report (V1.1 October 2020)                                      4/7
The daily data used in the modelling is being consolidated from measured real time data of the
independent variables available from calibrated and/or reliable independent data sources such as the
BOM climate data. A large number of possible independent variables have been identified and these
are being monitored during the baseline period prior to modelling commencing.



7A.5(d)
The End-User Equipment being measured in association with the RESA consists of the following;
    - The HVAC systems of each train set sampled.
        and
    - The 8 carriage train set as a whole (for its operational status)

The baseline regression model proposed will be developed to take into account the interrelationship
between the electricity consumption of the train set HVAC systems under the current control software
modes.



7A.5(e)
The measurement equipment that has been employed in the development of the Baseline Model is as
follows:
     - On board electricity meters associated with each HVAC unit on each train set.
     - BOM climate data
     - Train set run time data provided by Sydney Trains for each train set.



7A.5(f)
The electricity meters on each HVAC unit are identified by the ACP as being accurate to +/- 0.3%.
BOM data is considered to be accurate. The ACP has not specified the nominal accuracy at this stage
but this is not considered to be an issued for the modelling.

7A.5(f1)
The Baseline Measurement Period (365 days) is sufficient to capture the full range of normal
operating conditions likely to occur in a normal year.
7A.5(g)
No data points have been excluded from the Baseline Measurement period as of the time of writing
this preliminary report.

7A.5(h)
Following my review of the above items relating to clause 7A.5 of the ESS Rule, I am satisfied that the
Measurement Procedures adopted by the ACP so far for this RESA are appropriate to meet the
requirements of the ESS Rule.

Notes.
Clause 7A.20
Because of the very large number of train “sets” operating on the Sydney Trains network the ACP has
opted to apply the Sampling Method to establish the Baseline and Operating Energy Models for this
RESA. The following is a review of the requirements of the ESS rule in relation the clause 7A.20
7A.20 (a)
The population selected for sampling contains;

    (i)      the existing End User equipment




IPART Template - Preliminary M&V Professional Report (V1.1 October 2020)                            5/7
    (ii)     Will not impact on the services being provided
    (iii)    The RESA is a control software upgrade
    (iv)     The site constants have been nominated in the M&V plan
    (v)      N/A
7A.20 (b), (c), (d), (e), (f), (g) and (h)
Refer to section 6.4 of the M&V Plan.

In my opinion the sampling methods described in the M&V plan address all the requirements of
clause 7A.20 and the approach to be taken is aligned with these requirements of the ESS Rule. The
proposed sample size of 10 of each type of train set is more than 6 times the number of Site
Constants.

## Presentation - Dave Rees Rev C.pptx

*File: `Presentation - Dave Rees Rev C.pptx`*

### Slide 1
Energy Savings Funding ProgramforSydney Trains HVAC Upgrade
October 2020

### Slide 2
Context
The NSW Energy Savings Scheme (ESS) has the potential to deliver funding back to support proposed sustainability initiatives – in particular energy savings derived from some of the software and hardware upgrades to the Sydney train fleets.
It requires novel application of a framework, developed for fixed infrastructure, to mobile rolling stock, a new application of the ESS with some degree of risk and no precedent.
The application requires:
an approach that successfully models an increased number of complex variables 
satisfying ESS auditors and successfully managing the disposition of certificates on a live ESS trading platform
We have
identified a path to manage these factors, and retained a qualified ESC (Energy Saving Certificates) issuer as a partner
We have proposed a value share model in recognition of the innovation and uncertainty associated with the approach.
2

### Slide 3
Program Description
We have confirmed:

ApproachThe best approach is a ‘Measurement and Verification’ method treating the fleets as a series of tranches, defined by the number of sets that may be modified in each window prior to Winter or Summer
Brings forward the ESC payments (early as possible)
Allows for software and hardware modifications to be ‘assessed’ as tranches

Reference ModelCertificate issue requires the development of a baseline energy reference model for each tranche
Data from each set in the tranche post modification needs to be collected, and energy savings determined over both Summer and Winter
Normalisation for passenger loads is permitted
[See Next Slide]

### Slide 4
SGT Software (41)Verification Window 1
April 21 – Jan 22
Verification Plan Development, Model Development and approval

(Dec – March 21)
Data Analysis
Report & Audit
SGT SOFTWARE
FUNDS RECIVED AT MARKET
Program - Software Modifications
Engagement Started 
Completed
Awaiting TfNSW / Sydney Trains Nomination

June 21 – Feb 22

(Dec – March 21)

Report & Audit

(Dec – March 21)
Data Analysis

WARATAH Pt 2 SOFTWARE
INDIC FUNDS RECIVED
NET ~$1.7m

### Slide 6
Process Overview

### Slide 7
Risk Profile

### Slide 8
Modifications (8-10 sets)
Verification Window 1
Verification Plan Development and approval
(3 Months)
Mod fleet (tranche) selected, & reference data for tranche collected
Data Analysis
Report & Audit
Verification Window 2
Modifications (12 sets)
Verif’n Plan Update & approval
(1 Month)
Report & Audit
HARDWARE TRANCHE 1 FUNDS RECEIVED
Data Analysis
Program (Hardware Modifications)
Hardware Pilot
HARDWARE TRANCHE 2 FUNDS RECEIVED
Hardware pilot requested by TfNSW to demonstrate savings – does not contribute to ESS in anyway.
Indicative $5.1m Funds available to be applied to HVAC Hardware Modifications

### Slide 9
Engagement Approach | Detailed Scope Allocation
CBS Scope of services and fees
Management and delivery services - assigned project manager / engineer to: 
Work with Downer to collect and collate the required materials
Provide regular status reports to Downer and TfNSW if required

Certificate Management comprising
Authorised Certificate creation and trading
Government fees for certificate registration 
Compulsory Certificate audit costs

Scheme compliance
Measurement and verification report to ensure compliance to the PIAM&V method of the ESS scheme
Coordination and collation of all evidence and documentation from related stakeholders
Project definition documents, Eligibility check and Assignment form
Measurement and Verification 
Create pre-upgrade Baseline 
Multi-variable regression analysis to create baseline energy model
Evidence collection & collation
Multi-variable regression analysis to create operating energy model and create normalized operating energy consumption
Downer Scope of services and fees
Provision of data required to develop the baseline model and subsequent verification
HVAC power usage and passenger loading data
Modification documentation as required by the scheme

### Slide 10
CBS Group Certificate Issuer
Our chosen Certificate Issuer partner has provided incentive funding for energy efficiency and renewal for more than 35,000 Commercial, Industrial and Residential premises since its formation in 2012.

They are the leading issuer with the skills required for complex measurement and verification methods with 8 Certified Measurement and Verification Professionals (CMVPs)
Accreditations
Accredited provider under Victorian Energy Upgrades
Accredited Certificate Provider under the NSW ESS
Australian Carbon Credit Units Account Holder, under CER
Registered STC & Power Station agent, under CER
Together we can provide Downer access to NSW Energy Savings Certificates (ESCs), Victorian Energy Efficiency Certificates (VEECs) and Renewable Energy Target Small Technology Certificates (STCs) The Federal Renewable Energy Target and The Federal Carbon Farming Initiative





Shared Values
CBS Group and Partners believe in economics; that the deployment of investment and capital in the right places is the best way to drive change, and the greatest opportunity to address climate changes lies in the way the world generates and consumes energy.




### Slide 11
Open Issues and Questions
Start Date
To take advantage of the software upgrade, CBS will require a commencement date of no later than 30 Nov to be sure that SGT software modifications can be registered for ESCs.
Others?

## Presentation - Dave Rees Rev D.pptx

*File: `Presentation - Dave Rees Rev D.pptx`*

### Slide 1
Energy Savings Funding ProgramforSydney Trains HVAC Upgrade
October 2020

### Slide 2
Context
The NSW Energy Savings Scheme (ESS) has the potential to deliver funding back to support proposed sustainability initiatives – in particular energy savings derived from some of the software and hardware upgrades to the Sydney train fleets.
It requires novel application of a framework, developed for fixed infrastructure, to mobile rolling stock, a new application of the ESS with some degree of risk and no precedent.
The application requires:
an approach that successfully models an increased number of complex variables 
satisfying ESS auditors and successfully managing the disposition of certificates on a live ESS trading platform
We have
identified a path to manage these factors, and retained a qualified ESC (Energy Saving Certificates) issuer as a partner
We have proposed a value share model in recognition of the innovation and uncertainty associated with the approach.
2

### Slide 3
Program Description
We have confirmed:

ApproachThe best approach is a ‘Measurement and Verification’ method treating the fleets as a series of tranches, defined by the number of sets that may be modified in each window prior to Winter or Summer
Brings forward the ESC payments (early as possible)
Allows for software and hardware modifications to be ‘assessed’ as tranches

Reference ModelCertificate issue requires the development of a baseline energy reference model for each tranche
Data from each set in the tranche post modification needs to be collected, and energy savings determined over both Summer and Winter
Normalisation for passenger loads is permitted

## Program and Next Steps Brief Rev G.pptx

### Slide 1
Energy Savings Certificate Funding ProgramSydney Trains HVAC Upgrade
September 2020

### Slide 2
Context
The NSW Energy Savings Scheme (ESS) has the potential to deliver funding back to Downer based on software and hardware upgrades to the Sydney train fleets.
It requires application of a framework, developed for fixed infrastructure, to mobile rolling stock, a new application of the ESS.
The application requires:
an approach that successfully models an increased number of complex variables 
satisfying ESS auditors and successfully managing the disposition of certificates on a live ESS trading platform
To take advantage of the software upgrade, CBS requires a commencement date of no later than 30 Nov to be certain SGT software modifications are compliant for ESCs.
CBS has:
identified a path to manage these factors, and retained a qualified ESC (Energy Saving Certificates) issuer as a partner
We have proposed a value share model whereby Downer provides the data and pays CBS Group a recoupable retainer through project development
In response to Sean McCormack's request we have also costed a non-shared risk, T+M engagement
Shared risk/shared value is our preferred option.
It demonstrably delivers less risk and more value to Downer
However this requires executive sponsorship – hence our request for your review today to determine Downer's appetite for this proposition.
2

## Proposal for ESC creation from PIAM.docx

*File: `Proposal for ESC creation from PIAM.docx`*

Proposal for ESC creation from PIAM&V

Prepared for CBS Group Pty Ltd for Downer Sydney Trains HVAC Upgrade

CONTACT DETAILS

Hamish McGovern
hamish@wattly.com
0416 296 827

Alan Wang
alan@wattly.com
0434 126 577

9th September 2020

V1.0 - Valid for six weeks

Our Understanding

Sydney Trains is planning to implement and commission a range of Energy Conservation Measures (ECM) in the form of HVAC upgrades across the fleet of trains operating across the Greater Sydney Metropolitan Area. Downer will be performing these upgrades over a period of 4-5 years.  CBS Group is looking to access funding from the NSW Energy Savings Scheme (ESS) with minimal risk and effort, to provide additional capital and reduce the net cost for Downer throughout the upgrade cycle.  

The Energy Saving Certificates (ESCs) under the ESS provide incentive funding for large scale energy efficiency projects in NSW. The funding may be accessed yearly in arrears after the energy savings are realized using a Metered Baseline Method (MBM) or in a lump sum using forward creation of up to 10 years using the Project Impact Assessment with Measurement and Verification (PIAM&V) method.   The Sampling Method is a sub-method of PIAM&V that allows for multiple site ESC creation using a sampling method where there is a population of similar ECMs.  This method is the recommended option for Sydney Trains upgrades.

The exact value of ESCs to the project is uncertain as the ESC price at time of creation following the project, and the final quantity to be created are not fully known until the measurements and modelling has been performed. Nevertheless, for this project the value is substantial. 

Wattly holds the accreditations under the NSW ESS to create ESCs and has a range of forward-contracting options to hedge the ESC price that can help to firm up this value, or we can simply trade ESCs on the wholesale market at the time of creation. We have extensive experience in the measurement and verification techniques required for this process, and creation of ESCs in NSW.

For this project the following system details have been provided

Per Fleet Energy Savings:	26.6 GWH per year

Upgrade period:  Late 2020 to 2025	

Number of Fleets: 2

Estimated Number of ESCs: Up to 500,000 based on 10 years (discounted) forward creation

The wholesale ESC price has averaged around $22.00 for the past five years

Hence total funding over five years could be as higher as $11 M (minus fees for creation)

Lifetime for the upgraded HVAC equipment & train fleets:  At least 10 years

For this project the following technical details have been provided that will assist in the modelling 

Each train set as extensive data logged on every 10 minutes

Data includes energy used on the HVAC circuits

Data includes passenger load numbers measured through weight in each carriage's suspension

Several different fleets of train sets to be upgraded (Waratah A sets and SGT sets)

The fleets operate across the eight different lines making up the Sydney network

NSW Energy Saving Scheme - ESS

The NSW ESS reduces electricity consumption by creating financial incentives for busineese to invest in energy savings projects. Energy savings are achieved by Accredited Certificate Providers (ACPs such as Wattly) assisting businesses to upgrading or installing more efficient equipment.  Wattly registers a number of Energy Savings Certificates proportional to the energy savings achieved in the upgrade.  Wattly sells the certificates to Scheme Participants (Energy Retailers such as AGL, Origin, EA, etc) who have an obligation under the Scheme to meet energy savings targets.

Businesses performing the energy saving upgrades (the original energy savers), must legally nominate Wattly as the energy saver to give Wattly the right to register the Energy Saving Certificates.  

Energy Savings from the upgrade are "brought forward" for up to 10 years of operation and the energy savings for this period are registered as Energy Saving Certificates (ESC).  An ESC represents a nominal MWh (Megawatt hour) of energy saved.

Replaced equipment cannot be reused and must be permanently decommissioned.  This is to ensure that the old inefficient equipment is not used elsewhere after it is removed.

The Independent Pricing and Regulatory Tribunal of NSW (IPART) is both the Scheme Administrator and Scheme Regulator.

The business paying for the energy saving upgrade much nominate (sign the Nomination Form) BEFORE the energy saving upgrade is fully installed (commissioned)

The current legislated end date of the NSW ESS is the end of 2025.  The ESS has strong bipartisan support and the current government is expanding the program and rebrand it as the NSW Energy Security Safeguard.  Wattly expects that the program will be rolled forward to at least 2030.

Experts in M&V Energy Saving Certificates

Wattly along with our wholly owned consulting business, Northmore Gordon, has generated funding from energy savings certificates across NSW and VIC from a wide range of upgrade projects including;

Train fleet LED Lighting upgrades (using a sampling method)

Ventilation fan upgrades for a large metropolitan road tunnel

Gas, Biogas and Biomass boiler upgrade for many large energy users

Chiller upgrades for a nationwide poultry producer and numerous commercial buildings

Air blower upgrades from Yeast manufacturing

Data Centre upgrades to UPS, HVAC and Chillers at a number of large sites

Swirl Burner and Electric Arc Furnaces efficiency improvements for smelting

Range of measure for national paper, packaging and recycling company

City wide LED street lighting upgrade

And many more

Wattly has registered over 5M Environment Certificates

ESC creation method recommended

Wattly is accredited for a range of methods of ESC creation in NSW, and our role is to optimize the process by selecting the best method(s) for your situation. 

The PIAM&V Sampling Method will avoid performing Measurement & Verification (M&V) on every train set upgraded.  Note that HVAC has seasonal variation 12 months of data (before and after the upgrade) is the required measurement period to determine the energy savings.

The options for using this upgrade.

Perform Sampling Method M&V on a tranche of trains set to and claiming the energy savings for the population of trains in that tranche.  This method will allow shorter claiming periods and maintain claims under the 50,000 ESC limit

Perform Sampling Method M&V across the entire fleet and claim energy savings for train sets upgraded on a periodic basis (eg quarterly).  This method is desirable in that it may reduce the total amount of M&V, but will hit limitations on total ESC creation under the current guidelines.

Metered Baseline Method.  This method involves measuring the energy savings at the boundary of the train network and claiming the actual measure energy savings each year in arrears with a regression model accounting for production variations.  This method is likely to be difficult to implement due to an aggregated view of the entire network and the potential for unexplained variations in the network. 

At this stage option 1 above is the preferred option at this stage to ensure fastest time to funds whilst keeping M&V work to a minimum.  As further exploratory work is performed the other options will remain under consideration to ensure the option that is most efficient and provides the highest level of funding for Downer will be pursed.


The diagram below explains graphically how the regression analysis and modelling calculate what the energy usage would have been had the upgrade not occurred.  The energy savings is the difference between the model baseline and the reduced figure that is actual measured.  

Figure 1: How savings are calculated for a ‘Normal Year’ using modelling under the PIAM&V method.

ESC creation value

We estimate the following volume and value of the certificates for this project.


*the actual quantity may vary up or down depending on the strength of the modelling correlations, the performance of the ECM, and the years of forward creation (using a persistence model).

**ESC prices have historically varied substantially. The final price would be determined after creation of the ESCs.   Wattly can also offer hedging contracts to provide price certainty.

The following chart shows the ESC fees for the past 12 months.  The long-term average price has been approximately $22.00

Rolling tranche / batch upgrade 

The timeline below provided by CBS Group will allow a sampling M&V approach to be used on batches of train sets allowing the certificates to be realized as soon as possible and by limiting the number of trains sets per tranche avoid hitting the 50,000 forward creation limit for a single project.

Offer

Wattly is offering to forward-create ESCs for up to 10 years of energy savings (discounted in accordance with decay factors and the PIAM&V method).  Payment for ESC will be made in following the registration and sale of the ESCs.  This is estimated to be approximately 16 months after the upgrade of the sampled train sets in each tranche.

Payment, Fees and Term

Wattly is offering to forward-create ESCs for up to 10 years of energy savings (discounted in accordance with decay factors and the PIAM&V Sampling Method).

Payment:  Wattly will make payment upon registration and sale of the ESCs.  Wattly's fees are deducted from the ESC sale price prior to payment and hence there is no invoicing or payment of the fee as such.

The volume base pricing below is based on achieving the cumulative totals below.  The pricing will be maintained for the full period of the upgrade and does not reset.


Alternative to $9.00 ESC fee is a one-off project commence fee of $35,000 payable to Wattly at the start of the project.  This fee will contribute to the initial discovery and modeling of the PIAM&V Sampling Method model.  If this option is taken, then the $5.50 fee (or 23% fee) is applied for the first 50,000 ESCs registered.

Conditions

* Watttly fees include external auditor fees.

** Government fees have increased by $0.02 every two years

% fees are based on the ESC wholesale price achieved on the sale of the ESCs

All prices are exclusive of GST

Term of Exclusive Engagement:  to 2030 (ESS end date of 2025 expected to be extended)

Payment will be made upon receipt of an invoice with 60-day payment terms.

Wattly reserves the right to terminate the agreement or renegotiate if the data provided is insufficient to build a strong regression model of the energy savings in accordance with the ESS requirements.

ESC Hedging, Sale and Payment

Through Wattly's trading experience and access to the Environment Certificate markets we can offer hedging services for the ESC price.  On agreement with the customer, Wattly will offer hedging services for difference parcels throughout the duration of the project.  This allows for the lock-in of the ESC price for up to 18 months.

Key assumptions

Wattly is nominated as the Accredited Certificate Provider for the purpose of the Energy Savings Scheme

A Nomination Form is signed prior to commissioning and normal operation of the ECM

The project is found to have a statistically valid baseline energy model that can be used to predict future performance 

ESCs will be created when energy use within the measurement boundary has been measured for a period of up to 12 months (though we keep this period as short as possible)

If at any time after the date this Agreement is signed any legislation or scheme rules that apply to this Agreement are repealed, terminated or materially amended (“Change of Law Event”) and as a result no person or entity (including the Regulator) can legally create or Transfer ESCs or the ESC Contract Quantity, then the parties must meet in good faith as soon as practicable after becoming aware of the Change of Law Event to negotiate a replacement arrangement to preserve the parties’ financial position under this Agreement to the extent possible.

Scope of Services

Scope of services

Wattly’s services proposed for this project will include the following:

Government fees for certificate registration 

Appointment of a Wattly Upgrade Manager appointed to your project

Appointment of a Certified Measurement and Verification Professional to your project

Certificate creation and trading

Coverage of certificate processing audit costs (external)

Measurement and verification report to ensure compliance to the PIAM&V method of the ESS scheme

Coordination and collation of all evidence and documentation from related stakeholders

Specifically, for PIAM&V projects, our scope of work includes:

Project definition documents

Eligibility check

Assignment form

Assignment of Project manager 

(Upgrade Manager, Risk Manager, Monitoring and Measurement Manager)

Measurement and Verification Plan

Create pre-upgrade Baseline 

Pictures of old equipment

Metering arrangement

Asset register of devices within the measurement boundary

Baseline data

Multi-variable regression analysis to create baseline energy model

Implementation and commissioning data

Evidence of purchase of new equipment

Evidence of commissioning (commissioning report)

Evidence of safety for electrical work

Evidence of decommissioning (if applicable)

Written confirmation of the start date of normal operations

Post-upgrade data collection

Pictures of new equipment

Metering arrangement (if changed)

Asset register of devices within the measurement boundary (if changed)

Organise post-upgrade data

Multi-variable regression analysis to create operating energy model

Create normalized operating energy consumption

QA verification

Document register

Prepare energy savings report and quantity of ESC creation

Arrange for CMVP signoff

Manage ESC creation and sale process

Create ESCs in government registry

Trade ESCs in accordance with contract or market conditions.

Documents required in general:

About Us

This project will be delivered by a combination of staff from Wattly and Northmore Gordon.  Wattly manages Environmental Certificate Creation and Trading, whilst its wholly owned consulting business (Northmore Gordon) helps large energy users save energy and money through consulting expertise.

We have an established track record in certificate creation and trading across all the environment certificate programs in Australian (Energy Efficiency, Renewable, and Carbon)

Wattly has provided incentive funding for energy efficiency and renewables to over 35,000 Commercial, Industrial and Residential premises

We are the leading certificate Aggregator in Victoria

We have created over 3 million certificates, providing over $50 million in value back to our partners and customers

We have 25 staff ready to assist you 

We hold accreditations under

The NSW Energy Saving Scheme

The Victorian Energy Upgrades Program

The Federal Renewable Energy Target 

For both Small-scale Technology and Large-scale Generation Certificates

The Federal Carbon Farming Initiative

We have technical staff with experience in industrial, commercial and residential energy efficiency upgrades, renewable energy and carbon management

We have 8 Certified Measurement and Verification Professionals (CMVPs)

Staff

Overview of select staff likely to be involved in this project include

Alan Wang (B. Eng (Electrical & Electronics), CMVP)

Alan holds an honours degree in Electrical and Electronic Engineering from the University of Melbourne. He is an experienced technical manager skilled in energy efficiency project management. He is a Certified Measurement & Verification Professional (CMVP) by the Association of Energy Engineers and is also a Certified Salesforce Administrator and Developer. He initiated and completed the first M&V project under the Project Based Activities method under the VEU scheme in Victoria, and it was the first project in Victoria to have certificates granted. 

Andrew Clarke (B. Eng (Chem), CMVP)

Andrew has 7 years undergraduate and 22 years post graduate experience working in a variety of industries such as the steel and chemical industries. He has worked in a range of roles, including plant operations, management, process design and improvement.

Andrew has completed energy audits on a wide variety of manufacturing, resource and agricultural industries, and is contracted on an ongoing basis in the steel industry to facilitate and support Energy Management Programs and Energy Efficiency Opportunities Act compliance.

Andrew has also completed a number of Greenhouse Gas Assessments for resource development applications.

Trent Hawkins (B. Eng (Mech))

Trent Hawkins is a Mechanical Engineer with seven years’ experience in the sustainable energy field, specialising in energy efficiency technologies, solar PV consulting, and community scale renewable energy projects. Technical expertise includes Solar PV concept design; diverse experience and knowledge of energy efficiency technologies; Australian energy market operation and regulation; and feasibility of multi-site and megawatt scale renewable energy projects. 

Hamish McGovern (BSc BE Elec, MBA, MD Wattly)

Hamish is driven by innovation and a challenge. With over 5 years in energy efficiency & renewables and more than 20 years in IT, he has constantly searched and found new and better ways of doing things. Having worked in software development, systems integration, architecture, sales and product management in cloud, online service and IT infrastructure he is now leveraging these skills to help drive energy efficiency and carbon abatement. Hamish holds a degree in Electrical Engineering as well as a BSc and an MBA.  Hamish is responsible for all aspects of Wattly business including the certificate trading & hedging.

Craig Morgan (B. Eng (Mech), CMVP, MD Northmore Gordon)

Craig Morgan is a professional engineer with 20 years of experience in industrial energy efficiency and renewable energy systems with experience working in Australia, PNG, Malaysia, USA, and New Zealand. He holds a Bachelor Degree in Mechanical Engineering and is a Certified M&V Professional. Currently he is on the Board of three companies in the energy sector, and member of IPART’s panel of M&V professionals.

Craig has combined technical engineering capabilities with business management experience to provide customers with a rounded and practical approach to energy efficiency in sectors such as water utilities, mining, dairy, food processing and manufacturing. This has included:

Chris Warris (B. Eng (Mech/Mat), CMVP)

Chris has fifteen years post graduate experience working in a variety of industries including manufacturing, commercial buildings, chemical processing, mining, food and beverage, health & aged care and government.

Chris has worked on a range of energy and greenhouse gas projects including Greenhouse Gas Assessments for mining project development applications; Energy Audits of manufacturing facilities, food processing facilities, commercial office buildings, hospitals and aged care facilities; energy simulation modelling for BCA and NABERS Energy assessments; energy audits of large industrial plants and food processing facilities for the Energy Efficiency Opportunity Program; and Energy Management Program implementation.

Andrew Dawkins (B. Eng (Mechatronics), CMVP)

Andrew joined Wattly having undertaken postgraduate studies in Sustainable Energy. Andrew is passionate about sustainability, climate change, and sees energy efficiency and renewable technologies as key to Australia's transition to a sustainable future.  Hailing from Western Australia, Andrew has worked in the past in oil & gas and mining sectors.  Dissatisfaction working for these industries prompted him to relocate to Melbourne in 2014 in order to pursue an Engineering Masters of Sustainable Energy at RMIT University.

## Revised FE04 HVAC Software Proposal - Extract.docx

*File: `Revised FE04 HVAC Software Proposal - Extract.docx`*

FE04 –Software Energy Efficiency HVAC (SEE HVAC)

Executive Summary

The train’s Climate Control System (CCS) plays one of the most significant roles in influencing customer and crew comfort. The current HVAC units on the Waratah train have been proven in service for more than 10 years for both sound performance and reliability. However, energy used to operate the Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) system is a significant factor in the operational cost of revenue service trains. Reducing energy consumption has been identified as a critical opportunity within this Fleet Enhancement.

Downer, together with Sigma, have reviewed the operation of the HVAC seeking to reduce energy consumption of the system. Revenue service data logs were analysed and opportunities to save energy were identified. In this proposal, Software Energy Efficiency HVAC (SEE HVAC) enhancements have been identified that reduce energy consumption without significantly impacting passenger and crew comfort. Hardware Energy Efficiency HVAC (HEE HVAC) Enhancements have been proposed separate to this document.

These SEE HVAC enhancements include:

Duct heater optimisation: Relaxing the set-point control for passenger saloon duct heaters, and minimising their usage particularly during warmer temperatures;

HVAC setback mode: Enabling the HVAC to significantly relax temperature set points while the train is powered and not attended. Further opportunities to save energy during this period are identified in Section 7. 

Simulations and calculations were developed to predict quantitatively the energy saved from these enhancements. Based on these predictions, typical operational conditions, 15% energy savings could be realised across the fleet.  

Scope

This proposal outlines the program of work to complete the design and verification of the SEE HVAC design. Downer will:  

Prepare the software specification;

Provide updated HVAC software to allow proposed enhancements to be trialled with configurable parameters;

Provide updated eTIS software allowing the HVAC setback mode to be trialled;

Provide formal Engineering Change Request (ECR) documentation to install software temporarily for in-service trial in accordance with the SGT Configuration Management Plan;

Prepare and undertake tests and trial for the two distinct enhancements to verify the updates:

Achieve energy savings; 

Maintain customer and crew expectations for heating and cooling performance; 

Maintain technical performance;

Implement the HVAC software on the SGT fleet.

Energy Savings

The enhancement technical and operational benefits realise the energy saving benefits as below.

Table 1: Energy Saving Benefits

Key assumptions on the fleet energy saving predictions include:

Simulation total is based on average climatic data for Sydney region and does not consider variations between the city and western suburbs;

The model does not include pull-down, it assumed that the train is at the correct operating temperature at the start of service; 

Passenger loads are from existing operational data but in practice will vary between trains.

Technical Solution

Enhancement Objectives

The SEE HVAC is designed to seamlessly enhance the HVAC system, to achieve four key objectives:

The SEE HVAC solution can be implemented to Waratah and SGT with no change to the hardware configuration;

The energy savings can be proven quantitively via measuring on the Set;

There is no significant impact to customer and crew expectations for heating and cooling performance; 

The design improves or has no-impact to the HVAC reliability.

Downer is proposing two software enhancements that can be implemented with no change to the hardware design. They include:

Duct heater optimisation: Relaxing the set-point control for passenger saloon duct heaters, and minimising their usage particularly during warmer temperatures;

HVAC setback mode: Enabling the HVAC to significantly relax temperature set points while the train is powered and not attended.

Description of Enhancement

Duct Heater Optimisation

Each saloon area of the car has duct heaters used to balance the temperatures between each area (Upper Saloon, Lower Saloon, Vestibules). This ensures the difference in temperatures between areas is not significant. The intensity of each duct heater is controlled using continuously variable Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) 0% to 100%. If the temperature between each area differs from another by more than 0.1°C, the HVAC control system will begin adjusting the heating intensity of the duct heaters to balance the saloon temperatures, where the heater intensity is based on the difference between desired temperature and rate of change in temperature. 

Since the temperature between areas is rarely the same, the consequence for this strict control algorithm means that even when operating in warm ambient temperatures, duct heaters operate frequently to balance the temperatures, contributing to the energy consumed by the duct heaters and the energy required to cool the air on the train.

Downer proposes the duct heater control algorithm is modified. An example of the modification proposed is as follows:

Relax the duct heating control setpoint from 0.1°C to 1.0°C i.e. duct heaters will not attempt to balance the temperature unless the temperatures differ by 1.0°C or more;

Disable duct heaters if the outdoor temperature exceeds 30°C.

HVAC Setback Mode

In current operation – to allow immediate return to service particularly between timetable peaks and when the full fleet is not required – Sets are left powered when not in-service and not attended. When the train is not powered down or ‘stabled’, HVAC is left running to regulate the cab and saloon environment to meet minimum operating specifications as if passengers and crew were onboard. Since nobody is onboard to benefit from the conditioned air, energy is wasted to maintain temperature. The Waratah and SGT Sets already meet strict temperature requirements to pull up or down the temperature within a short period to ensure comfort is provided by the time the train enters service, therefore there is no need to maintain ideal temperatures when the train isn’t in use.

Downer proposes that the HVAC software is updated to feature an ‘HVAC Setback Mode’ that will be self-initiated if crew have not signed in for 20 minutes (or other configurable time). This will be after crew have removed their keys. In HVAC Setback Mode, saving energy will be achieved by:

Disabling duct heaters;

Adjusting the passenger saloon and Crew Cab set point temperature to be between 10°C and 30°C where the HVAC will remain in ventilation mode; i.e.:

If outdoor temperature falls below 10°C, HVAC shall regulate to 10°C; 

If temperature rises above 30°C HVAC will regulate to 30°C;

TDC Cab end HVAC to continue operating in default mode to maintain equipment temperatures (similar to Nightsafe mode).

eTIS software shall be updated to provide an output to the HVAC PLC to indicate when the driver and guard is not signed in and when the train is not set to Presentation Mode.

Testing and Trials

The test phase has been planned to accelerate the implementation of the software on the SGT Sets.

Approval

Downer and Sigma will work with TfNSW and Sydney Trains to develop parameters and ensure changes meet the system specification. With software prepared, the project will undertake static train tests. A report of the static test results will be provided with an Impact Assessment as part of a formal ECR additionally seeking approval to take the software to in-service trials. 

Timeline

The duct heater optimisation and HVAC setback will follow separate test timelines. This will allow the eTIS version implementation to not impact other trials and reduce risk to the overall project timeline. Each in-service trial will be separately implemented on different sets over multiple phases allowing individual benefits of each enhancement to be measured and prompt identification of any impact. 

An outline of the SEE HVAC test plan is provided in Figure 2. 

Figure 2: SEE HVAC Timeline

Test Descriptions

A description of each test and trial is provided in Table 4.

Table 4: Testing and trial descriptions

Opportunities

Fresh Air Fan Control

The HVAC fresh air fan primary purpose is it to supply sufficient fresh air to the train interior, especially when doors are closed. When the passenger doors open, conditioned air is pushed out of the train by the static pressure generated by the fresh air fans pushing air into the moderately sealed saloon. The static pressure equalises between the train interior and outside allowing fresh air to be introduced:

naturally through the passenger doors; and 

by the fresh air fans. 

When doors are then closed, the HVAC must consume energy to condition the air from both sources.

Downer proposes to reduce the speed of the fresh air fans for the passenger area when doors are open to reduce the amount of fresh air that requires conditioning and reduce the energy consumption of the fresh air fan. The fresh air fans will then return to normal operation after a short pre-defined period after the doors are closed.

Table 5: Potential enhancements considered during development of this proposal

During the development of this software enhancement Downer and Sigma identified some system constraints which resulted in this enhancement not being able to be offered through a simple HVAC software update. Downer will continue to investigate the feasibility of this enhancement, investigating whether fresh air fan control can be implemented without major on set modifications.

Future Considerations 

All equipment in the Climate Control System has been holistically reviewed for optimisation to realise the energy saving. Further energy efficiency could be gained in other areas as outlined in Table 6 below.

Table 6: Potential enhancements for future considerations.

Additional investigations could be undertaken by Downer subject to further time and cost consideration. 

Timeline

Table of Key Milestones

An approximate timeline of key milestones has been considered and outlined in Table 7 below. All dates and timelines are provisional and will be confirmed in the next phase once the enhancements are selected and the suppliers are formally agreed to meet the agreed delivery timeframe. 

Table 7: Indicative timetable

Appendix D – Prototype Setback Mode Test

An informal test was performed at Auburn Maintenance Centre to demonstrate energy savings for the Setback mode proposal. 

In this test a set was fully powered set left in normal mode and parked simulating the current baseline. The HVAC power consumed to-date was recorded to form a baseline followed by recordings every hour after to monitor the energy being consumed in this static, ‘powered’ state. 

The same test was performed with another Set, but in this test all HVAC units set to ventilation mode to simulate the newly proposed set-back mode. The same baseline and hourly power consumption recordings were taken in the ventilation mode. 

The comparative results are shown below:

Table 10 Prototype Setback Mode Test Energy Saving Results

The energy savings demonstrated by this test are significantly higher than the predicted calculation. However, this test didn’t fully simulate all seasonal scenarios, energy required to pull up or down the temperature when the Set returns to service or maintaining the temperature between 10°C and 30°C. While the predicted calculations are conservative, this test provides further evidence that significant energy savings can be achieved from the implementation of this enhancement.

Appendix E – HVAC Logs Demonstrating Duct Heater Operation

The following image is a snapshot of HVAC data logs taken from N1824 (B24) on 4th January 2020 demonstrating duct heater operation during ambient temperatures of approximately 45°C. The numbers in the duct heater column indicate the power level of the upper and lower deck heater heaters operating at up to 100% power.

Figure 4 Snapshot from N1824 HVAC data logs.

1               FE04 –Software Energy Efficiency HVAC (SEE HVAC)
1.1             Executive Summary
The train’s Climate Control System (CCS) plays one of the most significant roles in influencing customer
and crew comfort. The current HVAC units on the Waratah train have been proven in service for more
than 10 years for both sound performance and reliability. However, energy used to operate the Heating,
Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) system is a significant factor in the operational cost of revenue
service trains. Reducing energy consumption has been identified as a critical opportunity within this Fleet
Enhancement.
Downer, together with Sigma, have reviewed the operation of the HVAC seeking to reduce energy
consumption of the system. Revenue service data logs were analysed and opportunities to save energy
were identified. In this proposal, Software Energy Efficiency HVAC (SEE HVAC) enhancements have
been identified that reduce energy consumption without significantly impacting passenger and crew
comfort. Hardware Energy Efficiency HVAC (HEE HVAC) Enhancements have been proposed separate
to this document.
These SEE HVAC enhancements include:
§     Duct heater optimisation: Relaxing the set-point control for passenger saloon duct heaters, and
      minimising their usage particularly during warmer temperatures;
§     HVAC setback mode: Enabling the HVAC to significantly relax temperature set points while the
      train is powered and not attended. Further opportunities to save energy during this period are
      identified in Section 3.
Simulations and calculations were developed to predict quantitatively the energy saved from these
enhancements. Based on these predictions, typical operational conditions, 15% energy savings could be
realised across the fleet.

1.2             Scope
This proposal outlines the program of work to complete the design and verification of the SEE HVAC
design. Downer will:
§     Prepare the software specification;
§     Provide updated HVAC software to allow proposed enhancements to be trialled with configurable
      parameters;
§     Provide updated eTIS software allowing the HVAC setback mode to be trialled;
§     Provide formal Engineering Change Request (ECR) documentation to install software temporarily for
      in-service trial in accordance with the SGT Configuration Management Plan;
§     Prepare and undertake tests and trial for the two distinct enhancements to verify the updates:
      §   Achieve energy savings;
      §   Maintain customer and crew expectations for heating and cooling performance;
      §   Maintain technical performance;
§     Implement the HVAC software on the SGT fleet.

1.3             Energy Savings
The enhancement technical and operational benefits realise the energy saving benefits as below.

2               Technical Solution
2.1             Enhancement Objectives
The SEE HVAC is designed to seamlessly enhance the HVAC system, to achieve four key objectives:
1. The SEE HVAC solution can be implemented to Waratah and SGT with no change to the hardware
   configuration;
2. The energy savings can be proven quantitively via measuring on the Set;
3. There is no significant impact to customer and crew expectations for heating and cooling
   performance;
4. The design improves or has no-impact to the HVAC reliability.
Downer is proposing two software enhancements that can be implemented with no change to the
hardware design. They include:
§     Duct heater optimisation: Relaxing the set-point control for passenger saloon duct heaters, and
      minimising their usage particularly during warmer temperatures;
§     HVAC setback mode: Enabling the HVAC to significantly relax temperature set points while the
      train is powered and not attended.

2.2             Description of Enhancement
2.2.1           Duct Heater Optimisation
Each saloon area of the car has duct heaters used to balance the temperatures between each area
(Upper Saloon, Lower Saloon, Vestibules). This ensures the difference in temperatures between areas is
not significant. The intensity of each duct heater is controlled using continuously variable Pulse Width
Modulation (PWM) 0% to 100%. If the temperature between each area differs from another by more than
0.1°C, the HVAC control system will begin adjusting the heating intensity of the duct heaters to balance
the saloon temperatures, where the heater intensity is based on the difference between desired
temperature and rate of change in temperature.
Since the temperature between areas is rarely the same, the consequence for this strict control algorithm
means that even when operating in warm ambient temperatures, duct heaters operate frequently to
balance the temperatures, contributing to the energy consumed by the duct heaters and the energy
required to cool the air on the train.
Downer proposes the duct heater control algorithm is modified. An example of the modification proposed
is as follows:
§     Relax the duct heating control setpoint from 0.1°C to 1.0°C i.e. duct heaters will not attempt to
      balance the temperature unless the temperatures differ by 1.0°C or more;
§     Disable duct heaters if the outdoor temperature exceeds 30°C.

    Software Update      Description of update             Impact of update
                                                           + Significantly reduce overall duct heater
                                                           power consumption
                         Modify the duct heater control    + Reduce power required to cool saloon air
    Duct Heater          algorithm for temperature
    Optimisation         balancing during normal           - Slight increase in temperature difference
                         operation                         between passenger areas
                                                           o No impact to the average saloon temperature
                                                           and passenger comfort



© Downer 2020                                                                               Commercial in Confidence
2.2.2           HVAC Setback Mode
In current operation – to allow immediate return to service particularly between timetable peaks and
when the full fleet is not required – Sets are left powered when not in-service and not attended. When
the train is not powered down or ‘stabled’, HVAC is left running to regulate the cab and saloon
environment to meet minimum operating specifications as if passengers and crew were onboard. Since
nobody is onboard to benefit from the conditioned air, energy is wasted to maintain temperature. The
Waratah and SGT Sets already meet strict temperature requirements to pull up or down the temperature
within a short period to ensure comfort is provided by the time the train enters service, therefore there is
no need to maintain ideal temperatures when the train isn’t in use.
Downer proposes that the HVAC software is updated to feature an ‘HVAC Setback Mode’ that will be
self-initiated if crew have not signed in for 20 minutes (or other configurable time). This will be after crew
have removed their keys. In HVAC Setback Mode, saving energy will be achieved by:
§     Disabling duct heaters;
§     Adjusting the passenger saloon and Crew Cab set point temperature to be between 10°C and 30°C
      where the HVAC will remain in ventilation mode; i.e.:
     §    If outdoor temperature falls below 10°C, HVAC shall regulate to 10°C;
     §    If temperature rises above 30°C HVAC will regulate to 30°C;
§     TDC Cab end HVAC to continue operating in default mode to maintain equipment temperatures
      (similar to Nightsafe mode).
eTIS software shall be updated to provide an output to the HVAC PLC to indicate when the driver and
guard is not signed in and when the train is not set to Presentation Mode.

    Software Update      Description of update             Impact of update
                         HVAC Setback Mode is
                         activated after the driver has
    HVAC Setback         keyed out of the train. In this
                                                           + Reduce HVAC power consumption
    Mode                 mode, the HVAC will widen
                         setpoint temperatures and
                         disable duct heaters




© Downer 2020                                                                                Commercial in Confidence
2.3             Testing and Trials
The test phase has been planned to accelerate the implementation of the software on the SGT Sets.

2.3.1           Approval
Downer and Sigma will work with TfNSW and Sydney Trains to develop parameters and ensure changes
meet the system specification. With software prepared, the project will undertake static train tests. A
report of the static test results will be provided with an Impact Assessment as part of a formal ECR
additionally seeking approval to take the software to in-service trials.

2.3.2           Timeline
The duct heater optimisation and HVAC setback will follow separate test timelines. This will allow the
eTIS version implementation to not impact other trials and reduce risk to the overall project timeline.
Each in-service trial will be separately implemented on different sets over multiple phases allowing
individual benefits of each enhancement to be measured and prompt identification of any impact.
                              An outline of the SEE HVAC test plan is provided in




Figure 1.




                                         Figure 1: SEE HVAC Timeline

© Downer 2020                                                                           Commercial in Confidence
3               Opportunities
3.1             Fresh Air Fan Control
The HVAC fresh air fan primary purpose is it to supply sufficient fresh air to the train interior, especially
when doors are closed. When the passenger doors open, conditioned air is pushed out of the train by
the static pressure generated by the fresh air fans pushing air into the moderately sealed saloon. The
static pressure equalises between the train interior and outside allowing fresh air to be introduced:
      § naturally through the passenger doors; and
      § by the fresh air fans.
When doors are then closed, the HVAC must consume energy to condition the air from both sources.
Downer proposes to reduce the speed of the fresh air fans for the passenger area when doors are open
to reduce the amount of fresh air that requires conditioning and reduce the energy consumption of the
fresh air fan. The fresh air fans will then return to normal operation after a short pre-defined period after
the doors are closed.
                Table 3: Potential enhancements considered during development of this proposal
      Proposed Update             Description of update                    Impact of update
    Fresh air fan control     Reduce speed of the fresh        + Reduce power consumption of fresh air
                              air fan for a period after       fans6
                              passenger doors are opened       + Reduce power required to condition
                                                               fresh air
During the development of this software enhancement Downer and Sigma identified some system
constraints which resulted in this enhancement not being able to be offered through a simple HVAC
software update. Downer will continue to investigate the feasibility of this enhancement, investigating
whether fresh air fan control can be implemented without major on set modifications.

Appendix D – Prototype Setback Mode Test
An informal test was performed at Auburn Maintenance Centre to demonstrate energy savings for the
Setback mode proposal.
In this test a set was fully powered set left in normal mode and parked simulating the current baseline.
The HVAC power consumed to-date was recorded to form a baseline followed by recordings every hour
after to monitor the energy being consumed in this static, ‘powered’ state.
The same test was performed with another Set, but in this test all HVAC units set to ventilation mode to
simulate the newly proposed set-back mode. The same baseline and hourly power consumption
recordings were taken in the ventilation mode.
The comparative results are shown below:
                           Table 6 Prototype Setback Mode Test Energy Saving Results

                           Average Energy Saved      Per Set (MWh)      Per Unit (MWh)
                           Per hour                       0.194               0.012
                           Per 5 hours                    0.970               0.061
                           Per year7                       354                 22
The energy savings demonstrated by this test are significantly higher than the predicted calculation.
However, this test didn’t fully simulate all seasonal scenarios, energy required to pull up or down the
temperature when the Set returns to service or maintaining the temperature between 10°C and 30°C.
While the predicted calculations are conservative, this test provides further evidence that significant
energy savings can be achieved from the implementation of this enhancement.




7
    Annual energy saving based on Setback mode where duct heats are switched off for 5 hours
