This should be a back-to-back provision as we do not want a circumstance where ACP ‘run late’ on selling ‘because they can’ and we don’t meet our 6-month deadline on the transport side. 
DC – May be worth increasing this to 12 months (subject to CBS advice). We will need to confirm with NGE that they can comply with the requirements of clause 5.1 as per the latest amendments. This is in progress. |

|  | Cl 5.1(c) | Downer has requested clarification on the purpose of this clause, which relates to Downer providing information and reasonable access to the SGT Fleet to a representative of the ESS or a member of the ESS Audit Services Panel. | The template Nomination Form published on IPART's website provides that the original energy saver (TfNSW) agrees to provide information regarding the energy saving activities, and reasonable access to the implementation sites (SGT Fleet), to a representative of the ESS or a member of the ESS Audit Services Panel, upon their request. This clause was included in the Side Deed to pass this obligation through to Downer, to enable TfNSW to comply with its obligations in this respect.
Separately, the ACP will also have statutory obligations to provide information, assistance and access for monitoring, audit and reporting purposes. This obligation therefore also facilitates the ACP's compliance with its obligations. | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 5.4(e) and (f) | Firstly, in relation to TfNSW's right to revoke NGE's nomination as the ACP at any time, Downer has proposed that:
the nomination should cease at the end of the requirements for record-keeping, on the basis that if the nomination is revoked or expires earlier, this will invalidate the ESCs that were created under that nomination; and
revocation should only be for cause and under exceptional, irredeemable circumstances.
Secondly, Downer has deleted the reference to NGE as the ACP 'owning' all energy savings arising from the implementation of the HVAC Software Update. | In relation to the first point, we accept that revocation should only be for cause and under exceptional, irredeemable circumstances. We also agree that the nomination should cease at the end of the Term of the Side Deed, which is intended to cover record-keeping requirements (see our comments at item 15 below in relation to the Term). We have incorporated this drafting into clauses 5.4(e) and (f). 
For completeness, we note that the legislation does not deal with the consequences of a revocation of an energy saver nomination, and does not provide that ESCs created under that nomination will be invalidated as a result of such revocation.
In relation to the second point, the template Nomination Form published on IPART's website states that the nomination entitles the ACP to create and own all energy savings in respect of the energy saving activities. The 'ownership' language was included for this reason, and we have therefore retained it. | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 6.1(b) | Downer is proposing that it pay TfNSW the TfNSW Share of Proceeds within 30 BDs after the Payment Trigger Date (currently 2 BDs), noting that 2 BDs is practically unrealistic and that the payment terms should align with the SGT TLS Contract. | We have amended this in the revised draft of the Side Deed to be 10 Business Days. | DC – Downer’s position is 20 Business Days, in line with SGT payment terms. Especially given this is now linked to Termination, with only 5 business days grace. |

|  | Cl 7(a) | Downer has deleted the requirement that its monthly reports must be in a form acceptable to TfNSW, on the basis that this requirement is too generic and the minimum reporting requirements are well outlined in clause 7(a). 
Downer also notes that monthly reporting should be incorporated in the SGT TLSPPR. | We have incorporated this amendment into the revised draft of the Side Deed. | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 13 | Downer has noted clause 13 as for discussion with TfNSW, noting that whilst the intent of the clause is clear (to say that Downer bears scope and cost risk with regards to the ESS application process), it unintentionally introduces ambiguity around Downer’s ongoing obligation to continue pursuing the application in circumstances where the scheme application is unsuccessful. This is a real risk given the novel application and requires consideration. | We understand Downer's concern, and have included an additional provision to the effect that if Downer has complied with its obligations under clause 13 to take all steps and do all things necessary to meet the requirements for the ESS, and after doing so, it is of the view that ESCs cannot be created, then it will notify TfNSW and the parties may, by mutual agreement, terminate the Side Deed. | Commercial to comment. I think we are okay, as long as we have priced in undertaking step in clause 13 (iv) – (vi). 
DC – Okay closed. |

|  | Cl 15 | Downer has included an initial term that expires on the earlier of: (a) 4 years from execution; and (b) 12 months following the last distribution of Net ESC Sale Proceeds, with an option to extend by mutual agreement (although the option period has been left blank). | We have proposed an alternative 'Term' clause in the revised draft of the Side Deed (see clause 3). We are proposing that the Deed continues for so long as ESCs can be created in respect of the implementation of the HVAC Software Update and all reporting, record-keeping and audit obligations have been completed, up to a limit of 10 years (unless otherwise agreed by the parties). The 10-year cap is intended to reflect reporting, record-keeping and audit requirements. | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 16 | Downer has deleted TfNSW's right to terminate the Side Deed immediately by written notice if the SGT Project Delivery Deed or the SGT TLS Deed is terminated or expires. | Given the Amended Variation Order and ESS Variation Proposal are issued under the SGT TLS Deed, it is difficult to conceive how these (and the Side Deed) could stay on foot if the SGT TLS Deed is terminated. We have therefore not incorporated this amendment into the revised draft of the Side Deed. | Okay. Closed. |



|  | Clause Ref. | Topic | Description of Sydney Trains Amendment | Downer Response |

|  | Parties, definition of 'Related Entity', cl 5.4(b) and 18 | Counterparty to Side Deed | We have made TfNSW the counterparty to the Side Deed, rather than Sydney Trains, to align with legislative requirements. The counterparty to the Side Deed should be the entity that is the 'Purchaser' and 'Energy Saver' under the ESS Rule. This is the entity that purchases the services from Downer that enable the relevant energy savings to be made, which in our view is TfNSW, as the counterparty to the SGT TLS Deed with Downer under which the variation order for the implementation of the HVAC Software Update has been issued.
However, in the event that this changes, we have also included clause 5.4(b) to deal with a situation where the Purchaser or Energy Saver under the ESS Rule should instead be a Related Entity of TfNSW (Sydney Trains or Transport Asset Holding Entity of NSW (TAHE)), to ensure that the Side Deed is still given effect and the Related Entity is recognised as the Purchaser or Energy Saver, instead of TfNSW.
We have also made the following consequential changes, in light of the above:
deleted the previous Recital H, which is no longer relevant, as it applied to Sydney Trains as the counterparty; and
included a right for TfNSW to assign or novate the Side Deed. | DC – Okay, closed. |

|  | Cl 18 | Vesting of SGT Fleet in TAHE | The SGT Fleet, while currently owned by TfNSW, will be vested in TAHE at a future point in time. As such, we have included a right for TfNSW to assign or novate the Side Deed, to facilitate this future vesting. | DC – Okay, closed. |

|  | Definition of 'ACP Contract', 
cl 5.1(b)(i)(A) and 5.4(f)(ii) | ACP Contract | We consider that TfNSW will need to be a party to the contract between the ACP and Downer, to ensure that TfNSW receives the benefit of the ACP's appointment and to put in place arrangements for the ACP's nomination as energy saver by TfNSW, as well as for GST purposes to give effect to invoicing and remittance requirements. | We will need to confirm with NGE that they can comply with the requirements of clause 5.1 as per the latest amendments. This is in progress.
Regardless Downer cannot accept “on terms acceptable to TfNSW”. Downer has broad obligations under the proposed Side Deed and will ensure such obligations are discharged, including through our subcontractors / consultants where necessary. |

|  | Cl 4(b)(iii) and 4(d) | Timing for implementation of Amended Variation Order | As raised by Downer (see item 3 in the table above), on reflection we also thought that 'all other steps which are necessary to enable ESCs to be created' was too broad and may have led to uncertainty as to when this condition was satisfied. In the revised draft of the Side Deed, we have amended this to be 'all other steps reasonably required by TfNSW'.
To give the parties further certainty, we have also included a sunset date of 6 months within which the initial conditions set out in clauses 4(b)(i)-(iii) must be satisfied. If the initial conditions are not satisfied within this timeframe, TfNSW may terminate the Side Deed by notice to Downer and neither party will be liable to the other as a result of such termination. | DC – Okay, closed. |

|  | Cl 6.2(b) | No claims under SGT TLS Deed or SGT Project Delivery Deed | We have bolstered the language in this clause to reflect that the Side Deed is intended to be an entirely separate arrangement, distinct from both the SGT TLS Deed and the SGT Project Delivery Deed. Our amendments are intended to clarify that Downer will not be able to bring any claim or commence any dispute against TfNSW or its Related Entities under the SGT TLS Deed or SGT Project Delivery Deed, in respect of matters arising out of the Side Deed. | DC – Okay, but @Maria should this be reciprocal? |

|  | Cl 16(b) | Termination by TfNSW | We have included clause 16(b) to clarify that if TfNSW exercises any of its termination rights under clause 16(a), it will not be liable to Downer for any claim arising out of such termination. | DC – Okay, closed. |

|  | Definition of 'TfNSW's Representative', cl 19 | Notices and TfNSW's Representative | We have included a standard notices provision, which was missing from the previous draft of the Side Deed. TfNSW's Representative under the SGT TLS Deed should also be the recipient of notices to TfNSW under the Side Deed. | DC – Okay, closed. |

---


## Transport and Downer - ESS Side Deed Downer Comments - Sydney Trains Response(800744152.1).docx

*File: `ESS Nomination Form/Transport and Downer - ESS Side Deed Downer Comments - Sydney Trains Response(800744152.1).docx`*

ESS Side Deed Downer Comments (23 August 2022) – Sydney Trains Response

We set out in the table below our responses to Downer's comments on and requested amendments to the ESS Side Deed. We have not included the minor tidy up changes requested by Downer in the table below. These have been incorporated directly into the revised draft of the ESS Side Deed.

The clause references in the table below are to the clauses in the revised draft of the ESS Side Deed.

ESS Side Deed – Additional Amendments by Sydney Trains

In addition to the changes we have noted in the table above as being incorporated into the revised draft of the ESS Side Deed, we have also made the following amendments of note. We have not included minor tidy up changes (such as corrections to cross-references and definitions) in the table below. These have been incorporated directly into the revised draft of the ESS Side Deed.



|  | Clause Ref. | Downer Comment / Requested Amendment | Sydney Trains Response | Downer Response |

|  | Definitions of 'ACP's Fee' and 'Net ESC Sale Proceeds' | For simplicity:
the 'Supplier's Fee' should embed the ACP's Fee and all other associated costs; and
the 'Net ESC Sale Proceeds' should be calculated as the ESC Sale Proceeds minus the Supplier's Fee (rather than the ACP's Fee). | The proposed amendments suggest that the ACP will pass through to Downer the total amount of ESC Sale Proceeds (without deduction for the ACP's Fee), with Downer to then repay the ACP for its costs.
Our drafting was based on the assumption that, as the ACP would be receiving the ESC Sale Proceeds, it would keep 30% of those proceeds to cover its ACP's Fee, and then pay the balance to Downer, from which Downer would then keep the Supplier's Fee and pay the balance to TfNSW. We understand this is how the payments are intended to flow in practice, and the drafting should therefore reflect this. On this basis, we have not incorporated this amendment into the revised draft of the Side Deed. | DC - Okay, closed. |

|  | Definition of 'Supplier's Fee' | In response to TfNSW's query as to what is covered by the Supplier's Fee that is over and above what is covered by the ACP's Fee, Downer has advised that the Supplier's Fee broadly covers the activities outlined in clauses 5.1(b) (particularly (iii) to (viii)), 7 and 8.
Downer will provide an estimate breakdown in dollars, however the Supplier's Fee will be converted to a % of ESC Sale Proceeds, for consistency with this Deed (and the principle of TfNSW holding no risk as to costs). | [*]
[Note: TfNSW to confirm whether a % of ESC Sale Proceeds for the Supplier's Fee is acceptable.] | DC – Downer to provide further information by 7th September. |

|  | Cl 4(b)(iii) | Downer has requested that the conditions to be satisfied before the Amended Variation Order is implemented includes all other reasonably required steps which are necessary to enable the ESCs to be created. | We have incorporated this 'reasonably required' drafting, however we have made this all other steps reasonably required by TfNSW (see our comments at item 20 below). | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 5.1(b)(i)(C) | In implementing the ESS Variation Proposal, Downer is required to appoint a Scheme Auditor. Downer notes that the Scheme Auditor is only appointed once the relevant evidence is provided. | We agree with Downer's comment, and had not included a timeframe on this obligation for this reason.
In the revised draft of the Side Deed, we have clarified this obligation by including the words 'in accordance with the requirements of, and within the timeframe prescribed by, the Act, Regulation or Rule (as applicable)'. | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 5.1(b)(ii) | In implementing the ESS Variation Proposal, Downer is required to procure that NGE takes the steps set out in this clause. Downer has amended this obligation to procuring that NGE use reasonable endeavours to take those steps. | This amendment effectively converts NGE's obligations from strict obligations to only reasonable endeavours obligations, and therefore is not acceptable to Sydney Trains. We have not incorporated this amendment into the revised draft of the Side Deed. | DC - We will need to confirm with NGE that they can comply with the requirements of clause 5.1 as per the latest amendments. This is in progress. |

|  | Cl 5.1(b)(ii)(A) | On advice of its ESS Consultant that this step is not required, Downer has deleted the requirement that NGE applies to the Scheme Administrator as soon as reasonably practicable after the Effective Date to become accredited as a certificate provider in relation to the implementation of the HVAC Software Update as a Recognised Energy Saving Activity. | Based on the current list of ACPs published on IPART's website, NGE is already registered as an ACP in respect of a number of RESAs. The Act is clear that an ACP may only create ESCs in relation to those activities for which it has been accredited. It may be the case that NGE considers that its current accreditation covers the implementation of the HVAC Software Update, such that it does not need to apply to the Scheme Administrator to amend its accreditation to cover this activity.
In any event, we have amended this step to say that to the extent that NGE is not already accredited in relation to the implementation of the HVAC Software Update as a RESA, NGE must become accredited for this activity. | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 5.1(b)(ii)(B) | Downer must procure that NGE creates ESCs promptly after the energy savings occur and in any event within the timeframe prescribed by the Act, Regulation or Rule (as applicable). 
Downer has deleted 'promptly after the energy savings occur', as in its view, ESCs are not created promptly. Downer notes that the current program requires 12 months of data, plus modelling and auditor approval (approx. 12-18 months in total). | We have replaced 'promptly' with 'as soon as reasonably practicable', to account for the practical modelling and audit requirements.
For completeness, we note that the Act states that ESCs may be created by no later than 6 months after the end of the year in which the energy savings occur. | Okay. Closed 
DC - Okay |

|  | Cl 5.1(b)(ii)(D) | Downer has requested clarification of the requirement that NGE applies to the Scheme Administrator for registration of the creation of the ESCs, noting that the act of 'registering' is what creates the ESCs. | Our reading of the Act is that the act of 'creating' an ESC is separate from, and different to, the registration of that ESC. Specifically, s46 of Sch 4A to the Act states that:
an ESC has no force or effect until its creation is registered by the Scheme Administrator in the register of ESCs; and
an application for registration of the creation of an ESC may be made to the Scheme Administrator by an ACP.
There are also a number of provisions in the Act that refer to 'creation' only, without 'registration'. Our drafting was intended to reflect this distinction.
In any event, based on Downer's view that the act of registering the ESCs is what creates them, both obligations (to create and register) should be satisfied by a single act.
We have not made any changes to the revised draft of the Side Deed in this respect. | DC - We will need to confirm with NGE that they can comply with the requirements of clause 5.1 as per the latest amendments. This is in progress. |

|  | Cl 5.1(b)(ii)(E) | Downer must procure that NGE sells the ESCs promptly after the registration of the ESCs, and maximises the price received for the ESCs. Downer has flagged this point as for discussion with TfNSW, noting that 'promptly selling' may not 'maximise the price' as it potentially floods the market. | We accept the deletion of the word 'promptly', however we have included a cut-off date of 6 months (or such other timeframe as may be agreed by TfNSW) by which the ESCs must be sold. We consider that this is a sufficient period of time to address the market flooding issue. | Commercial to comment. Is 6 months sufficient for us? Does our consultant agreement also give a cut-off to ACP, for selling off the certificates? This should be a back-to-back provision as we do not want a circumstance where ACP ‘run late’ on selling ‘because they can’ and we don’t meet our 6-month deadline on the transport side. 
DC – May be worth increasing this to 12 months (subject to CBS advice). We will need to confirm with NGE that they can comply with the requirements of clause 5.1 as per the latest amendments. This is in progress. |

|  | Cl 5.1(c) | Downer has requested clarification on the purpose of this clause, which relates to Downer providing information and reasonable access to the SGT Fleet to a representative of the ESS or a member of the ESS Audit Services Panel. | The template Nomination Form published on IPART's website provides that the original energy saver (TfNSW) agrees to provide information regarding the energy saving activities, and reasonable access to the implementation sites (SGT Fleet), to a representative of the ESS or a member of the ESS Audit Services Panel, upon their request. This clause was included in the Side Deed to pass this obligation through to Downer, to enable TfNSW to comply with its obligations in this respect.
Separately, the ACP will also have statutory obligations to provide information, assistance and access for monitoring, audit and reporting purposes. This obligation therefore also facilitates the ACP's compliance with its obligations. | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 5.4(e) and (f) | Firstly, in relation to TfNSW's right to revoke NGE's nomination as the ACP at any time, Downer has proposed that:
the nomination should cease at the end of the requirements for record-keeping, on the basis that if the nomination is revoked or expires earlier, this will invalidate the ESCs that were created under that nomination; and
revocation should only be for cause and under exceptional, irredeemable circumstances.
Secondly, Downer has deleted the reference to NGE as the ACP 'owning' all energy savings arising from the implementation of the HVAC Software Update. | In relation to the first point, we accept that revocation should only be for cause and under exceptional, irredeemable circumstances. We also agree that the nomination should cease at the end of the Term of the Side Deed, which is intended to cover record-keeping requirements (see our comments at item 15 below in relation to the Term). We have incorporated this drafting into clauses 5.4(e) and (f). 
For completeness, we note that the legislation does not deal with the consequences of a revocation of an energy saver nomination, and does not provide that ESCs created under that nomination will be invalidated as a result of such revocation.
In relation to the second point, the template Nomination Form published on IPART's website states that the nomination entitles the ACP to create and own all energy savings in respect of the energy saving activities. The 'ownership' language was included for this reason, and we have therefore retained it. | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 6.1(b) | Downer is proposing that it pay TfNSW the TfNSW Share of Proceeds within 30 BDs after the Payment Trigger Date (currently 2 BDs), noting that 2 BDs is practically unrealistic and that the payment terms should align with the SGT TLS Contract. | We have amended this in the revised draft of the Side Deed to be 10 Business Days. | DC – Downer’s position is 20 Business Days, in line with SGT payment terms. Especially given this is now linked to Termination, with only 5 business days grace. |

|  | Cl 7(a) | Downer has deleted the requirement that its monthly reports must be in a form acceptable to TfNSW, on the basis that this requirement is too generic and the minimum reporting requirements are well outlined in clause 7(a). 
Downer also notes that monthly reporting should be incorporated in the SGT TLSPPR. | We have incorporated this amendment into the revised draft of the Side Deed. | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 13 | Downer has noted clause 13 as for discussion with TfNSW, noting that whilst the intent of the clause is clear (to say that Downer bears scope and cost risk with regards to the ESS application process), it unintentionally introduces ambiguity around Downer’s ongoing obligation to continue pursuing the application in circumstances where the scheme application is unsuccessful. This is a real risk given the novel application and requires consideration. | We understand Downer's concern, and have included an additional provision to the effect that if Downer has complied with its obligations under clause 13 to take all steps and do all things necessary to meet the requirements for the ESS, and after doing so, it is of the view that ESCs cannot be created, then it will notify TfNSW and the parties may, by mutual agreement, terminate the Side Deed. | Commercial to comment. I think we are okay, as long as we have priced in undertaking step in clause 13 (iv) – (vi). 
DC – Okay closed. |

|  | Cl 15 | Downer has included an initial term that expires on the earlier of: (a) 4 years from execution; and (b) 12 months following the last distribution of Net ESC Sale Proceeds, with an option to extend by mutual agreement (although the option period has been left blank). | We have proposed an alternative 'Term' clause in the revised draft of the Side Deed (see clause 3). We are proposing that the Deed continues for so long as ESCs can be created in respect of the implementation of the HVAC Software Update and all reporting, record-keeping and audit obligations have been completed, up to a limit of 10 years (unless otherwise agreed by the parties). The 10-year cap is intended to reflect reporting, record-keeping and audit requirements. | Okay. Closed. |

|  | Cl 16 | Downer has deleted TfNSW's right to terminate the Side Deed immediately by written notice if the SGT Project Delivery Deed or the SGT TLS Deed is terminated or expires. | Given the Amended Variation Order and ESS Variation Proposal are issued under the SGT TLS Deed, it is difficult to conceive how these (and the Side Deed) could stay on foot if the SGT TLS Deed is terminated. We have therefore not incorporated this amendment into the revised draft of the Side Deed. | Okay. Closed. |



|  | Clause Ref. | Topic | Description of Sydney Trains Amendment | Downer Response |

|  | Parties, definition of 'Related Entity', cl 5.4(b) and 18 | Counterparty to Side Deed | We have made TfNSW the counterparty to the Side Deed, rather than Sydney Trains, to align with legislative requirements. ## Aggregated-Metered-Baseline-Application-Form-Part-B-ACPs-V3.0.docx

*File: `ACP_PART B (Measures)/Aggregated-Metered-Baseline-Application-Form-Part-B-ACPs-V3.0.docx`*

Application for Accreditation – Part B

Method Details – MBM – AMB

	Purpose of this form

This form is used to apply for accreditation as an Accredited Certificate Provider or ‘ACP’ for the Metered Baseline Method – Aggregated Metered Baseline (AMB) sub-method.  It should be accompanied by Application Form – Part A (general details) and other supporting documentation, as indicated in these forms. 

Refer to the AMB Method Guide to assist you in completing this form.  Essential information about the requirements of applicants and ACPs is set out in the Application for Accreditation Guide, Record Keeping Guide, and the Guide to Accredited Certificate Provider’s Obligations.  Your application information must be consistent with and meet the requirements set out in these documents.  More information on the application process can be found on the ESS website.

	Meaning of key terms, icons and colours in this form

Key terms

ACP means accredited certificate provider.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you will be an ACP. 

ESC means energy savings certificate.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you may be able to create ESCs from the activities conducted under the RESA.

ESS Rule means the Energy Savings Scheme Rule of 2009.  The ESS Rule sets out the specific requirements of each calculation method under the ESS.  Requirements specific to AMB are set out in clause 8.9 of the ESS Rule.  The ESS Rule is available on the ESS website.

EUE means end-user equipment. EUE is equipment, processes, or systems which consume electricity, gas, or a combination of electricity and gas. This includes the equipment directly consuming electricity and/or gas as well as equipment or products that cause, control or influence the consumption of electricity and/or gas.

Implementation means the delivery of a RESA at a site.

RESA means recognised energy saving activity.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you will be accredited in respect of a specific energy saving activity.

Site means the location of the EUE included in a RESA.

Icons

	Indicates an instruction for completing this form.

	Indicates a document or supporting evidence to be provided with the application.

	Important information to assist you with completing the application.

Shaded questions

Blue shaded questions must be completed by all applicants.

Amber shaded questions must only be completed if relevant to the application.

	How to complete and submit your application

Complete Application for Accreditation - Part A and Application for Accreditation - Part B (this form).  

	Finalise supporting documentation.

	Review the completed application forms and supporting documentation to ensure that they are concise, complete and accurate.  This is an opportunity to demonstrate the effectiveness of your quality assurance procedures to IPART.

	Submit the application forms and supporting documentation to IPART.

Method eligibility

Which of the following activities will be included in the proposed RESA?

Select one or more of the activities that will be included in the RESA.

Which accredited statistician have you engaged?

The statistician must be accredited by the Statistical Society of Australia Inc and be accepted by the Scheme Administrator.

How will you allocate sites to the treatment group or control group?

Attachment

Provide verification (in writing and with reasoning) from an accredited statistician that the unbiased selection method is valid.

Are any ineligible activities included in the proposed RESA?

The following activities are not eligible RESAs:

The installation of T5 adaptor kits or Retrofit Luminaire-LED Linear Lamps

Activities to meet mandatory statutory or regulatory requirements

Activities that are a Standard Control Service or Prescribed Transmission Service, except if the activity is a Non-Network Option (Network Service Providers only)

The purchase of electricity under “Green Power” or similar schemes

Activities with negative effects on production or service levels (including safety levels)

If any of these activities are part of your RESA you need to explain how they will be excluded from your energy savings and ESC calculations.

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How will you exclude ineligible activities from the RESA?

End-user equipment

Will you be modifying, replacing or installing EUE?

If your proposed activity does not involve modifying, replacing or installing EUE, it will not be a RESA and will not eligible for accreditation under the ESS.

Select one or more of the activities that will be included in the RESA.

What EUE will be included in the proposed RESA?

Include all relevant end-user equipment.

Will you be installing new EUE (where there is no existing EUE)?

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How does the energy efficiency of the new EUE compare to other EUE that provides the same function?

New EUE installed as part of the proposed RESA must have a greater energy efficiency than the average energy efficiency of other EUE that provides the same type of function, output or service.  Refer to clause 5.3B of the ESS Rule.

Will you be replacing EUE?

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly

How will you ensure that EUE removed as part of the proposed RESA is not refurbished, reused or resold?  

Equipment that is removed as part of the RESA must be disposed of appropriately. It must not be refurbished, re-used or resold.

How will you manage disposal of EUE that is removed as part of the implementation?  

Lighting equipment containing mercury removed during upgrades in metropolitan levy areas (refer Table A25 of the ESS Rule) must be recycled in accordance with the requirements of a Product Stewardship Scheme. 

If EUE containing refrigerants is removed as part of an upgrade, the refrigerants must be disposed of in a manner that is compliant with the Ozone Protection and Synthetic Greenhouse Gas Management Act 1989.

How will you maintain production or service levels?

RESAs must not result in a reduction of production and/or service levels (including safety levels).  This means that the performance or outputs of the site must not be reduced as a result of the RESA. 

Energy data

How will you collect and record energy data?

Are you using metered energy data held by an energy retailer or network service provider?

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

What metering arrangements are in place for each site?

If you are not using metering data, you must notify the Scheme Administrator of any metering arrangement you intend to use and obtain written notice from the Scheme Administrator that the proposed metering arrangement meets the requirements of the ESS Rule.

Attachment

Provide evidence of compliance with the accuracy requirements of the National Measurement Institute document M6 (Electricity Meters).

How will you account for sites with measured energy consumption for only part of the implementation period?

Calculation of energy savings

How will you calculate the observed energy savings?

Attachment

Provide verification (with reasoning) from an accredited statistician that the analysis method is valid.

How will you ensure the accuracy of energy savings and ESC calculations?

You must have appropriate procedures for checking calculations of energy savings and ESCs.  

If during an audit the auditor identifies errors in calculations from which ESCs have been registered, you may be requested, or required, to forfeit any improperly created ESCs.

Record Keeping Guide
Energy Savings Scheme

Independent Pricing and Regulatory Tribunal of New South Wales
PO Box K35, Haymarket Post Shop NSW 1240
Level 15, 2-24 Rawson Place, Sydney NSW 2000
T (02) 9290 8400   F (02) 9290 2061
www.ipart.nsw.gov.au


                                                     Record Keeping Guide IPART   iii
     Document Control

     Version           Change description                                    Date published
     V1.1              Initial release of Record Keeping Guide               9 January 2012

     V2.1              Record Keeping Guide was updated to:                  16 June 2015
                          better identify what records are required to be
                           kept by Accredited Certificate Providers to
                           support ESC creation by prescribing
                           accreditation conditions that refer to the
                           evidence manuals published on our website,
                           and
                        better communicate our requirements for

                       Manual for some methods.




iv   IPART Record Keeping Guide
Contents




1   Introduction                                                                1

2   Legal requirements for record keeping under the Energy Savings
    Scheme                                                                      2

3   Record keeping requirements under the Energy Savings Scheme                 3
    3.1 Records to be kept                                                      3
4   Record keeping arrangements                                                 4
    4.1 Document register                                                       4
    4.2 Processes and/or procedures for keeping records                         5
    4.3 Quality assurance – controls to ensure records are accurate             6

5   Continuing arrangements for record keeping                                  6

                                                        Record Keeping Guide IPART   v
1        Introduction
The NSW Energy Savings Scheme (ESS) seeks to reduce energy consumption in
NSW by creating financial incentives for organisations to invest in energy saving
projects.

The other objects of the ESS are to:
   assist households and businesses to reduce energy costs
   make the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions achievable at a lower cost,
    and
   reduce the cost of, and need for, additional energy generation, transmission
    and distribution infrastructure. 1

Electricity retailers and other mandatory participants (Scheme Participants) are
obliged to meet energy saving targets. Energy savings are achieved by installing,
improving or replacing energy saving equipment. Companies that become
Accredited Certificate Providers (ACPs) can create energy savings certificates
(ESCs) from these activities and then sell those ESCs to Scheme Participants. The
Independent Pricing and Regulatory Tribunal of NSW (IPART) is both the
Scheme Administrator and Scheme Regulator of the ESS. 2

Collecting and retaining the required records to support the methodology, data
and assumptions used to calculate energy savings is an accreditation condition
for ACPs and is critical for the proper creation of ESCs. In order to comply with
this condition of accreditation, ACPs must have complete records before the
creation of ESCs. ESCs created without the required supporting records may be
found to be improperly created.

Section 3 of the Record Keeping Guide (Guide) identifies the records that ACPs
are required to keep. Section 4 of this Guide sets out the Scheme Administrator
requirements for appropriate record keeping arrangements. Sections 3 and 4 of
this Guide are incorporated as requirements in an ACP’s accreditation notice.

This Guide is also a useful reference for applicants who are preparing their
application for accreditation. Applicants should make sure that all the
requirements mentioned in this Guide are adequately addressed in any
application for accreditation.

This Guide should be used in conjunction with the ESS Application Guide 3, the
Method Guides 4, Evidence Manuals 5 and Evidence Packs 6 relevant to your
accreditation or your application.

1   Electricity Supply Act 1995, section 98(2)
2   Electricity Supply Act 1995, sections 153(2) and 151(2) respectively
3   See www.ess.nsw.gov.au/How_to_apply_for_accreditation
4   See www.ess.nsw.gov.au/Methods_for_calculating_energy_savings
5   See www.ess.nsw.gov.au/Methods_for_calculating_energy_savings



                                                                     Record Keeping Guide IPART   1
    The Scheme Administrator may update this Guide from time to time.
    Notification of updates to this Guide will be published at www.ess.nsw.gov.au.



    2       Legal requirements for record keeping under the
            Energy Savings Scheme
    One of the eligibility requirements for accreditation as an ACP in respect of your
    activity is that your record keeping arrangements have been approved by the
    Scheme Administrator, or that the Scheme Administrator is satisfied that you will
    have appropriate record keeping arrangements in place at the time of
    implementation.

    Once you are accredited and carrying out your activity, you are required to keep
    specific records in order to support your ESC creation. These records are specific
    to the activity for which you are accredited and are identified in section 3 of this
    Guide.

    It is a condition of accreditation that you collect and retain these records and to
    ensure these records are complete prior to the creation of ESCs. Compliance with
    this requirement will be assessed through audit. A failure to keep the required
    records may be a breach of your accreditation conditions, which could result in a
    range of penalties including suspension or cancellation of your accreditation.

    Table 1 provides a summary of the key record keeping requirements in the
    Electricity Supply (General) Regulation 2014 (the Regulation). It is a summary only
    and does not constitute legal advice.

    Table 1        Record keeping requirement for the ESS
    Requirement                                                             Regulation clause
    The person is eligible for accreditation if the person has record keeping Clause 38(1)
    arrangements with respect to the activity approved by the Scheme
    Administrator or (for a proposed activity) the Scheme Administrator is
    satisfied that the person will have appropriate record keeping
    arrangements in respect of that activity when the activity is carried out.
    The ACP must keep a record of:                                          Clause 46(1)
     the location in which the activity occurred,
     the energy savings (calculated in accordance with the scheme
      rules) arising from that activity, and
     the methodology, data and assumptions used to calculate those
      energy savings.
    An ACP must keep any records that the Scheme Administrator has, by Clause 46(2)
    notice in writing to the ACP, required the ACP to keep.




    6   See www.ess.nsw.gov.au/Methods_for_calculating_energy_savings



2   IPART Record Keeping Guide
Requirement                                                               Regulation clause
A record required to be kept by a person must be retained by the          Clause 46(3)
person for at least 6 years after it is made.
Records are to be kept in a form and manner approved by the Scheme Clause 46(4)
Administrator.




3       Record keeping requirements under the Energy
        Savings Scheme
This section sets out the Scheme Administrator’s standard requirements
regarding records that must be kept by ACPs. You will need to maintain record
keeping arrangements that meet the Scheme Administrator’s requirements as
long as you are accredited under the Scheme and will need to retain all records
for a period of 6 years after ESC creation, whether or not you are accredited.


3.1     Records to be kept

You are required to collect and retain the evidence identified in the guidance
document relevant to the calculation methodology for which you are accredited
as listed in Table 2 below. The guidance documents are published on the ESS
website at www.ess.nsw.gov.au. We will update these documents and develop
new guidance from time to time.

Table 2        Evidence required to be kept
Method                                           Guidance document
Deemed Energy Savings Method – Sale of           Method Guide - Sale of New Appliances
New Appliances
Deemed Energy Savings Method –                   Evidence Manual – Commercial Lighting
Commercial Lighting Energy Savings               Energy Savings Formula
Formula
Deemed Energy Savings Method – High              Method Guide – High Efficiency Motors
Efficiency Motors Energy Savings Formula         Energy Savings Formula
Deemed Energy Savings Method – Power             Method Guide – Power Factor Correction
Factor Correction Formula                        Formula
Deemed Energy Savings Method – Removal           Method Guide – Removal of Old Appliances
of Old Appliances
Deemed Energy Savings Method –                   Method Guide – Installation of High Efficiency
Installation of High Efficiency Appliances for   Appliances for Business
Business
Deemed Energy Savings Method – Home              Method Guide – Home Energy Efficiency
Energy Efficiency Retrofits                      Retrofits
Metered Baseline Method - NABERS                 Method Guide - NABERS baseline
baseline




                                                                  Record Keeping Guide IPART      3
    Method                                       Guidance document
    Metered Baseline Method – Method 1,          Method Guide – Metered Baseline Method
    Method 2 and Method 3

    It is an ACP’s responsibility to ensure they comply with the most recent version
    of the relevant guidance document at the time of implementing an activity and
    creating ESCs. Auditors will audit compliance using the version of the guidance
    document that was current at the time of your ESC creation.

    There are several methods for which records will be proposed and assessed at the
    time of application for accreditation, rather than specified in detail in the
    guidance documents. The proposed records will be approved as part of your
    accreditation. These methods include:
       Aggregated Metered Baseline
       Metered Baseline Method (Methods 1, 2 and 3), and
       Project Impact Assessment with Measurement and Verification.




    4       Record keeping arrangements
    ACPs must maintain appropriate record keeping arrangements to ensure that
    they are able to collect the evidence required. You must ensure that you have
    record keeping arrangements that will:
       facilitate the collection of the required records
       allow accurate records to be provided to the Scheme Administrator or to an
        auditor at any time, and
       retain the records for at least 6 years after ESC creation.


    4.1     Document register

    You must have a document register that lists the records that must be collected
    and retained to support your ESC creation. For most methods, this list has been
    provided in the method guide relevant to your accreditation (see Table 2).

    Where a list of evidence has not been developed, your document register must
    identify the evidence that will be collected and retained to support ESC creation.
    The evidence required will depend on the calculation methodology for which
    you are accredited but in general, you should include:
       nomination form, where you are to be nominated as the energy saver




4   IPART Record Keeping Guide
   details of the location of the Recognised Energy Saving Activity 7 (RESA)
   evidence to show that the RESA has been, or will be, implemented eg, sales
    invoices
   evidence to support inputs to the calculation of energy savings
   records of the calculation of energy savings arising from the RESA
   records of the calculation of the number of ESCs to be created
   records that demonstrate that the RESA remains implemented and
    functioning for the life of the proposed energy savings, and
   any other records identified as part of your application for accreditation.


4.2      Processes and/or procedures for keeping records

You need to explain the step-by-step details of how records will be obtained,
processed, maintained and controlled as well as identify the position(s) of the
person(s) responsible for each of these steps in a process/procedure document.

Applicants with multiple business units involved in the RESA may have separate
(but interlinked) record keeping processes for each business unit.

Record keeping processes and procedures must include:
   a description of where data was sourced, how the data is identified and
    recorded (within the relevant information systems)
   how each record in the document register is created, approved and updated
   details of any information systems, databases, and/or spreadsheets used to
    collate, manage or store records listed in the document register
   how each record in the document register is identified (via the record
    identification protocol/naming protocols) and where each record is located
   the position of the person responsible for each type of record in the document
    register
   processes for archiving and retrieving documents, and
   a description of how records will be retained for a minimum of six years.

In support of the above requirements, you need to have a process map at the time
of application that identifies and illustrates the key steps and platforms for the
flow of a record from its initiation to its eventual archival.




7   A Recognised Energy Saving Activity (RESA) is an activity that reduces electricity consumption in
    respect of which ESCs may be created.



                                                                     Record Keeping Guide IPART         5
    4.3     Quality assurance – controls to ensure records are accurate

    You must demonstrate that you have adequate mechanisms in place to ensure
    that the records are complete, accurate and reliable. You must also show the
    processes and responsibilities for checking records, especially calculations and
    steps in the creation and registration of ESCs.

    To do this, you should provide a quality assurance document describing:
       the documented control procedures in place for each record
       details of any internal audit and reconciliation procedures you have
        developed to support your record keeping
       the procedure for checking energy savings calculations prior to creation and
        registration of ESCs, and
       the position of the person who is responsible for performing these actions.




    5       Continuing arrangements for record keeping
    Once you have been accredited for an activity, you will be issued with an
    accreditation notice listing conditions that have been imposed by the Scheme
    Administrator.      Some of those accreditation conditions may relate to
    requirements for record keeping. You must comply with all accreditation
    conditions. You should read your accreditation notice and contact the ESS team
    if you do not fully understand the requirements.

    You are also responsible for ensuring that your record keeping arrangements
    continue to meet any additional Scheme Administrator requirements, as
    described in Sections 3 and 4 of this Guide. These requirements may be updated
    from time to time and you will be asked to update your record keeping
    arrangements accordingly.

    Your record keeping arrangements will be tested at audit, as prescribed in your
    accreditation notice, or as required by the Scheme Administrator. The Scheme
    Administrator may also require you to provide copies of your record keeping
    documentation for review at any time.




6   IPART Record Keeping Guide
A      Australian Standard on Records Management
It is strongly suggested that record keeping arrangements be consistent with the
Australian Standard on Records Management AS/ISO 15489:2002. This provides
guidance on developing auditable record keeping procedures. You should apply
this Standard when developing and maintaining your record keeping
arrangements. It is published by Standards Australia International Ltd
(Standards Australia) and copies can be purchased from the Standards Australia
website - www.standards.com.au.

You may also find it useful to refer to the:
   Australian Standard on Environmental Management Systems AS/NZS ISO
    14001:2004 for specific guidance on environmental records; and
   Australian Standard on Quality Management Systems AS/NZS ISO 9001:2008
    for guidance on developing auditable record keeping procedures.

If your organisation is accredited under any of these standards, please provide
evidence in your application for accreditation.




                                                       Record Keeping Guide IPART   7

---


## MBM-Application-Form-Part-B-ACPs-V3.2.docx

*File: `ACP_PART B (Measures)/MBM-Application-Form-Part-B-ACPs-V3.2.docx`*

Application for Accreditation – Part B

Method Details – Metered Baseline Methods

	Purpose of this form

This form is used to apply for accreditation as an Accredited Certificate Provider or ‘ACP’ for the following sub-methods of the Metered Baseline Method (MBM):

method 1 – baseline per unit of output, 

method 2 – baseline unaffected by output, and 

method 3 – normalised baseline. 

It should be accompanied by Application Form – Part A (general details) and other supporting documentation, as indicated in these forms.

The MBM Method Guide will assist you in completing this form.  Essential information about the requirements of applicants and ACPs is set out in the:

ACP Application Guide, and 

Record Keeping Guide.  

Your application information must be consistent with and meet the requirements set out in these documents.  More information on the application process can be found on the ESS website.

	Meaning of key terms, icons and colours in this form

Words which are defined in the ESS Rule and used in this Application Form have the same meaning in this Application Form as in the ESS Rule, unless the context requires otherwise.

Key terms

ACP means accredited certificate provider.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you will be an ACP. 

ELT means emerging lighting technology.  Products classified as ELTs must be accepted for use by IPART.  

ESC means energy savings certificate.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you may be able to create ESCs from the activities conducted under the RESA.

ESS Rule means the Energy Savings Scheme Rule of 2009.  The ESS Rule sets out the specific requirements of each calculation method under the ESS.  Requirements specific to MBM are set out in clauses 8.5, 8.6 and 8.7 of the ESS Rule.  The ESS Rule is available on the ESS website.

EUE means end-user equipment.  EUE is equipment, processes, or systems which consume electricity, gas, or a combination of electricity and gas.  This includes the equipment directly consuming electricity and/or gas as well as equipment or products that cause, control or influence the consumption of electricity and/or gas.  

Implementation means the delivery of a RESA at a site.

RESA means recognised energy saving activity.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you will be accredited in respect of a specific energy saving activity.

Site means the location of the EUE included in a RESA.

Icons

	Indicates an instruction for completing this form.

	Indicates a document or supporting evidence to be provided with the application.

	Important information to assist you with completing the application.

Shaded questions

Blue shaded questions must be completed by all applicants.

Amber shaded questions must only be completed if relevant to the application.

	How to complete and submit your application

Complete Application for Accreditation - Part A and Application for Accreditation - Part B (this form).  

Finalise supporting documentation.

Review the completed application forms and supporting documentation to ensure that they are concise, complete and accurate.  This is an opportunity to demonstrate the effectiveness of your quality assurance procedures to IPART.

Submit the application forms and supporting documentation to IPART.

Method eligibility – all MBM sub-methods

Which MBM sub-method(s) are you applying for?

Select the MBM sub-methods to be included under the RESA.

You must provide a worked example of a completed project for each MBM sub method you are applying for. The worked example will be used to assess your understanding of the MBM sub method and experience in delivering MBM projects.

Are you applying for multiple MBM sub-methods?

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How will you determine which MBM sub-method to use?

Each MBM sub-method has different eligibility criteria as outlined in the appendices of the MBM Method Guide. 

If you are applying for multiple MBM sub-methods, you must have a procedure in place to determine which sub-method to use for each implementation.

Attachment

Provide the documented procedure, with a flow diagram, that shows how you will determine which MBM sub−method to use.

Provide a worked example showing how the procedure applies in practice.

Which of the following activities will be included in the proposed RESA?

Select the activities to be included in the RESA.

Does the proposed RESA include fuel switching activities?

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How will you ensure there is no net increase in greenhouse gas emissions?

Full fuel cycle emissions factors and equations are found in the latest version of the National Greenhouse Accounts Factors.

Are any ineligible activities included in the proposed RESA?

The following activities are not eligible RESAs:

The installation of T5 adaptor kits or Retrofit Luminaire-LED Linear Lamps

Activities to meet mandatory statutory or regulatory requirements

Activities that are a Standard Control Service or Prescribed Transmission Service, except if the activity is a Non-Network Option (Network Service Providers only)

The purchase of electricity under “Green Power” or similar schemes

Activities with negative effects on production or service levels (including safety levels)

Activities eligible to create RECs (under the Commonwealth RET)

Activities that increase the consumption of non-renewable energy (other than gas)

Activities that will increase gas consumption efficiency but result in flaring of gas

Generation of electricity (except where used to provide equivalent goods or services with a generator that has a nameplate rating of less than 30 MW and does not export to the Electricity Network)

Activities that will increase greenhouse gas emissions, including fuel switching

If any of these activities are part of your RESA you need to explain how they will be excluded from your energy savings calculations.

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How will you exclude ineligible activities from the RESA?

RESA boundaries and metering 

How will you define the boundary of the proposed RESA?

Refer to section 3.4 of the MBM Method Guide.

Refer to your response to question 6 in Application Form Part A.

Attachment

Provide a drawing or schematic to demonstrate the measurement boundary, indicating energy consumption measurement points.

What metering equipment and processes are in place for each site?

Refer to section 3.4 of the MBM Method Guide.

End-user equipment 

Which activities will be conducted under the proposed RESA?

Select one or more of the activities that will be included in the RESA.

What EUE will be included in the proposed RESA?

Include all relevant end-user equipment.

Will you be undertaking a lighting upgrade?

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How will you meet ELT requirements?

ELTs such as LEDs and induction luminaires must meet certain safety requirements.  ELTs used in lighting upgrades must be accepted by IPART as meeting these requirements before ESCs can be created and registered from the use of ELTs in a lighting upgrade. Refer to the Lighting Requirements page of the ESS website for more information. 

Will you be installing new EUE (where there is no existing EUE)?

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How does the energy efficiency of the new EUE compare to other EUE that provides the same function?

New EUE installed as part of the proposed RESA must have a greater energy efficiency than the average energy efficiency of other EUE that provides the same type, function, output or service.  Refer to clause 5.3B of the ESS Rule.

Will you be removing or replacing EUE?

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How will you ensure that EUE that is removed as part of the proposed RESA is not refurbished, reused or resold?

Equipment that is removed as part of the RESA must be disposed of appropriately.  It must not be refurbished, re-used or resold.  Refer to clause 5.3A of the ESS Rule.

How will you manage disposal of EUE that is removed as part of the implementation?  

Lighting equipment containing mercury removed during upgrades in metropolitan levy areas (refer Table A25 of Schedule A to the ESS Rule) must be recycled in accordance with the requirements of a product stewardship scheme. 

If EUE containing refrigerants is removed as part of an upgrade, the refrigerants must be disposed of in a manner that is compliant with the Ozone Protection and Synthetic Greenhouse Gas Management Act 1989. 

How will you maintain production or service levels?

RESAs must not result in a reduction of production or service levels (including safety levels).  This means that the performance or outputs of the site must not be reduced as a result of the RESA. 

What process will you follow when the baseline of an implementation reaches the 10 year maximum limit?

Energy savings can only be calculated for a maximum of 10 years from the end date of the baseline measurement period. 

Include all relevant end-user equipment.

MBM sub-method 1 – baseline per unit of output

Are you applying to use MBM sub-method 1 – baseline per unit of output?

Refer to Appendix A of the MBM Method Guide.

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How will you verify that energy consumption is a linear function of output?

In order to use MBM sub-method 1, the energy consumption of a site must be a linear function of output, ie, energy consumption is strongly linked to the output from a site (eg, electricity consumption from aluminium smelting).

How will you select the baseline period and measurement periods Tb and Ta?

Refer to section 2 and Appendix A of the MBM Method Guide for a definition and schematic of the baseline period, and measurement periods Tb and Ta.

Attach a separate document if more space is required.

Attachment

Attach a separate document if more space is required to answer this question.

How will you measure or estimate fixed energy consumption?

How will you measure or estimate variable energy consumption?

How will you measure production output?

How will you measure or calculate total energy consumption?

How will you calculate net energy savings?

Net energy savings is the sum of electricity savings and gas savings.

Attachment

Provide the calculation spreadsheet(s) / tool(s) used to calculate energy savings. The spreadsheet / tool must show the steps and formulae used to calculate energy savings.

Provide a worked example showing how the baseline period, measurement periods Tb and Ta, fixed and variable energy consumption, production outputs, total energy savings and net energy savings are calculated.

How will you ensure the accuracy of energy savings and ESC calculations?

You must have appropriate procedures for checking calculations of energy savings and ESCs. 

If during an audit the auditor identifies errors in calculations from which ESCs have been registered, you may be requested, or required, to forfeit any improperly created ESCs.

MBM sub-method 2 – baseline unaffected by output

Are you applying to use MBM sub-method 2 – baseline unaffected by output?

Refer to Appendix B of the MBM Method Guide.

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

Will the RESA apply to production or service sites?

How will you verify that energy consumption remains unaffected by output?

In order to use MBM sub-method 2, all energy consumption should be independent of output from a production or service site, ie, energy consumption remains similar regardless of how many units are produced or how many people are served.

How will you select the baseline period and measurement periods Tb and Ta?

Refer to section 2 and Appendix B of the MBM Method Guide for a definition and schematic of the baseline period and measurement periods Tb and Ta.

Attach a separate document if more space is required.

How will you measure or calculate total energy consumption?

How will you calculate net energy savings?

Net energy savings is the sum of electricity savings and gas savings.

Attachment

Provide the calculation spreadsheet(s) / tool(s) used to calculate energy savings. The spreadsheet / tool must show the steps and formulae used to calculate energy savings.

Provide a worked example showing how the baseline period, measurement periods Tb and Ta, total energy savings and net energy savings are calculated.

How will you ensure the accuracy of energy savings and ESC calculations?

You must have appropriate procedures for checking calculations of energy savings and ESCs. 

If during an audit the auditor identifies errors in calculations from which ESCs have been registered, you may be requested, or required, to forfeit any improperly created ESCs.

MBM sub-method 3 – normalised baseline 

Are you applying to use MBM sub-method 3 – normalised baseline?

Refer to Appendix C of the MBM Method Guide.

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How will you determine the statistical correlation between energy consumption and normalisation variables?

In order to use MBM sub-method 3, variations to energy consumption must be able to be removed through normalisation against some other factor(s), ie, there is a statistical correlation between energy consumption and the normalisation variables.

How will you select the baseline period and measurement periods Tb and Ta?

Refer to section 2 and Appendix C of the MBM Method Guide for a definition and schematic of the baseline period, and measurement periods Tb and Ta.

Attach a separate document if more space is required.

Attachment

Attach a separate document if more space is required to answer this question.

How will you determine normalisation variables?

How will you measure or calculate total energy consumption?

How will you calculate net energy savings?

Net energy savings is the sum of electricity savings and gas savings.

Attachment

Provide the calculation spreadsheet(s) / tool(s) used to calculate energy savings.  The spreadsheet / tool must show the steps and formula used to calculate energy savings.

Provide a worked example showing how the baseline period, measurement periods Tb and Ta, normalisation variables, total energy savings and net energy savings are calculated.

How will you ensure the accuracy of energy savings and ESC calculations?

You must have appropriate procedures for checking calculations of energy savings and ESCs. 

If during an audit the auditor identifies errors in calculations from which ESCs have been registered, you may be requested, or required, to forfeit any improperly created ESCs.

## NABERS-Application-Form-Part-B-ACPs-V4.2.docx

*File: `ACP_PART B (Measures)/NABERS-Application-Form-Part-B-ACPs-V4.2.docx`*

Application for Accreditation – Part B

Method Details – MBM – NABERS

	Purpose of this form

This form is used to apply for accreditation as an Accredited Certificate Provider or ‘ACP’ for the Metered Baseline Method – NABERS Baseline sub-method.  It should be accompanied by Application Form – Part A (general details) and other supporting documentation as indicated in these forms. 

Refer to the NABERS Method Guide to assist you in completing this form.  Essential information about the requirements of applicants and ACPs is set out in the:

ACP Application Guide, and

Record Keeping Guide. 

Your application information must be consistent with and meet the requirements set out in these documents.  More information on the application process can be found on the ESS website.

	Meaning of key terms, icons and colours in this form

Words which are defined in the ESS Rule and used in this Application Form have the same meaning in this Application Form as in the ESS Rule, unless the context requires otherwise.

Key terms

ACP means accredited certificate provider.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you will be an ACP. 

ESC means energy savings certificate.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you may be able to create ESCs from the activities conducted under the RESA.

ESS Rule means the Energy Savings Scheme Rule of 2009.  The ESS Rule sets out the specific requirements of each calculation method under the ESS.  Requirements specific to NABERS are set out in clause 8.8 of the ESS Rule.  The ESS Rule is available on the ESS website.

EUE means end-user equipment. EUE is equipment, processes, or systems which consume electricity, gas, or a combination of electricity and gas. This includes the equipment directly consuming electricity and/or gas as well as equipment or products that cause, control or influence the consumption of electricity and/or gas.

Implementation means the delivery of a RESA at a site.

RESA means recognised energy saving activity.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you will be accredited in respect of a specific energy saving activity.

Site means the location of the EUE included in a RESA.

Icons

	Indicates an instruction for completing this form.

	Indicates a document or supporting evidence to be provided with the application.

	Important information to assist you with completing the application.

Shaded questions

Blue shaded questions must be completed by all applicants.

Amber shaded questions must only be completed if relevant to the application.

	How to complete and submit your application

Complete Application for Accreditation - Part A and Application for Accreditation - Part B (this form).  

Finalise supporting documentation.

Review the completed application forms and supporting documentation to ensure that they are concise, complete and accurate.  This is an opportunity to demonstrate the effectiveness of your quality assurance procedures to IPART.

Submit the application forms and supporting documentation to IPART.

Method eligibility

What are the details of the NABERS rated buildings?

Provide the site address and NABERS rating period for each NABERS building for which you intend to calculate energy savings for the first time.  If you don’t have any NABERS buildings, leave this question blank. 

Which NABERS calculation method will you use?

Select which calculation method(s) you intend to use.

You may select more than one calculation method.

How will you check that the NABERS rating is the first rating ever issued for a NABERS building?

How will you check that the NABERS rating is not issued to comply with any relevant statutory or regulatory instrument of any jurisdiction?

How will you check that the fixed Historical Baseline NABERS Rating has a similar configuration to that of the current NABERS rating?  

Are any ineligible activities included in the proposed RESA?

The following activities are not eligible RESAs:

The installation of T5 adaptor kits or Retrofit Luminaire-LED Linear Lamps

Activities to meet mandatory statutory or regulatory requirements

The purchase of electricity under “Green Power” or similar schemes

Activities that are a Standard Control Service or Prescribed Transmission Service, except if the activity is a Non-Network Option (Network Service Providers only)

Activities eligible to create RECs (under the Commonwealth RET)

Activities with negative effects on production or service levels (including safety levels)

Activities that will increases consumption of non-renewable energy (other than gas)

Activities that will increase greenhouse gas emissions, including fuel switching.

If any of these activities are part of your RESA you need to explain how they will be excluded from your energy savings and ESC calculations.

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How will you exclude ineligible activities from the proposed RESA?

How will the proposed RESA improve energy efficiency?

Select all that apply.

Calculation of energy savings

What approach will you use to calculate energy savings for each NABERS building?

Select all that apply.

How will you summarise calculations for each NABERS building?

Attachment

Provide the summary template for your NABERS building(s).

How you will identify all sources of on-site electricity generation?

How will you identify any sub-meters installed in a NABERS building?

How will you ensure the accuracy of energy savings and ESC calculations?

You must have appropriate procedures for checking calculations of energy savings and ESCs. If during an audit of your RESA the auditor identifies errors in calculations from which ESCs have been registered, you may be requested, or required, to forfeit any improperly created ESCs. 



| Address of NABERS building | NABERS rating period | NABERS rating period |

| Address of NABERS building | Start date | End Date |

|  |

|  |

|  |

|  |

|  |

|  |

|  |

|  |

|  |

|  |



|  | Calculation Method 1 – Table A20 in Schedule A of the ESS Rule (go to Questions 3 and 4) |

|  | Calculation Method 2 – Historical Baseline NABERS rating (go to Question 5) |



| Describe the process to check whether the NABERS rating is the first rating ever issued for a NABERS building |



| Describe the process to check whether the NABERS rating was issued to comply with any relevant statutory or regulatory instrument |



| Describe the process to check whether the Historical Baseline NABERS Rating has a similar configuration to that of the NABERS rating period used to calculate energy savings: |



|  | No | Go to Question 8 |

|  | Yes | Provide a response to Question 7 |



| Describe how any ineligible activities will be excluded from energy savings and ESC calculations: |



|  | Increasing the efficiency of electricity consumption |

|  | Increasing the efficiency of gas consumption, where gas is combusted for stationary energy |

|  | Fuel switching from electricity to gas |

|  | Fuel switching from gas to electricity |

|  | Generating electricity where the electricity is used to provide equivalent goods or services resulting in an overall reduction in energy consumption |



|  | Annual creation – Calculation Method 1 only |

|  | Annual creation – Calculation Method 2 only |

|  | Annual creation – Calculation Method 1 and Calculation Method 2 |

|  | Forward creation only |

|  | Forward creation and top-up |

|  | Forward creation, top-up and annual creation thereafter |



| Based upon your answer to Question 9, describe how you will summarise your calculations for each NABERS building. |



| File name – summary template used when bundling NABERS buildings: |



| Describe how you will identify all sources of on-site electricity generation when these are not accounted for by the NABERS rating: |



| Describe how you will identify any sub-meters installed in a NABERS building, for the purpose of meeting the similar configuration criteria: |

## PFC-Application-Form-Part-B-ACPs-V3.0 (1).docx

*File: `ACP_PART B (Measures)/PFC-Application-Form-Part-B-ACPs-V3.0 (1).docx`*

Application for Accreditation – Part B

Method Details – PFC

	Purpose of this form

This form is used to apply for accreditation as an Accredited Certificate Provider or ‘ACP’ for the Power Factor Correction Energy Savings Formula (PFC) sub-method.  It should be accompanied by Application Form – Part A (general details) and other supporting documentation as indicated in these forms. 

Refer to the PFC Method Guide to assist you in completing this form.  Essential information about the requirements of applicants and ACPs is set out in the Application for Accreditation Guide, Record Keeping Guide and the Guide to Accredited Certificate Provider’s Obligations.  Your application information must be consistent with and meet the requirements set out in these documents.  More information on the application process can be found on the ESS website.

	Meaning of key terms, icons and colours in this form

Key terms

ACP means accredited certificate provider.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you will be an ACP. 

ESC means energy savings certificate.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you may be able to create ESCs from the activities conducted under the RESA.

ESS Rule means the Energy Savings Scheme Rule of 2009.  The ESS Rule sets out the specific requirements of each calculation method under the ESS.  Requirements specific to PFC are set out in clause 9.6 of the ESS Rule.  The ESS Rule is available on the ESS website.

EUE means end-user equipment. EUE is equipment, processes, or systems which consume electricity, gas, or a combination of electricity and gas. This includes the equipment directly consuming electricity and/or gas as well as equipment or products that cause, control or influence the consumption of electricity and/or gas.  

Implementation means the delivery of a RESA at a site.

RESA means recognised energy saving activity.  If this application for accreditation is approved, you will be accredited in respect of a specific energy saving activity.

Site means the location of the EUE included in a RESA.

Icons

	Indicates an instruction for completing this form.

	Indicates a document or supporting evidence to be provided with the application.

	Important information to assist you with completing the application.

Shaded questions

Blue shaded questions must be completed by all applicants.

Amber shaded questions must only be completed if relevant to the application.

	How to complete and submit your application

Complete Application for Accreditation - Part A and Application for Accreditation - Part B (this form).  

	Finalise supporting documentation.

	Review the completed application forms and supporting documentation to ensure that they are concise, complete and accurate.  This is an opportunity to demonstrate the effectiveness of your quality assurance procedures to IPART.

	Submit the application forms and supporting documentation to IPART. If your application is successful, you will be approved to install EUE.

Method eligibility

Are any ineligible activities included in the proposed RESA?

The following activities are not eligible RESAs:

Activities to meet mandatory statutory or regulatory requirements

Activities that are a Standard Control Service or Prescribed Transmission Service, except if the activity is a Non-Network Option (Network Service Providers only)

Activities with negative effects on production or service levels (including safety levels)

Activities eligible to create RECs (under the Commonwealth RET)

If any of these activities are part of your RESA you need to explain how they will be excluded from your energy savings and ESC calculations.

Check the appropriate box and respond accordingly.

How will you exclude ineligible activities from the RESA?

End-User Equipment

How will you ensure that PFC installations meet equipment requirements?

How will you ensure the PFC equipment is installed in accordance with relevant electrical and safety standards?  

How will you maintain production or service levels?

RESAs must not result in a reduction of production or service levels (including safety levels). This means that the performance or outputs of the site must not be reduced as a result of the RESA. 

Calculation of energy savings

How will you determine the power savings for each implementation?

The power savings formula is set out in clause 9.6 of the ESS Rule and the appendices of the Method Guide.  

How will you calculate the energy savings for each implementation?

The power savings formula is set out in clause 9.6 of the ESS Rule and the appendices of the method guide. Inputs to the power savings formula may be updated from time to time.

Attachment

Provide the calculation spreadsheet(s) / tool(s) you will use to calculate energy savings. The spreadsheet / tool must show the steps and formulae used to calculate energy savings

How will you ensure the accuracy of energy and ESC calculations?

You must have appropriate procedures for checking calculations of energy savings and ESCs.  If during an audit the auditor identifies errors in calculations from which ESCs have been registered, you may be requested, or required, to forfeit any improperly created ESCs.



|  | No | Go to Question 3 |

|  | Yes | Provide a response to Question 2 |



| Describe how any ineligible activities will be excluded from energy savings calculations: |



| Describe how you will ensure that the PFC will be supplied through capacitors: |

| Describe how you will ensure the PFC capacitors installed at the site are new capacitors (and not second hand or reused capacitors): |
